---
name: generic-design-system
description: Complete design system reference for any project - colors, typography, spacing, components, animations. Adapts to project theme and tech stack. Use when implementing UI, choosing colors, creating animations, or ensuring brand consistency.
---

# Generic Design System

Design system reference adapting to any project's visual language.

**Foundational Reference:** See [Design Patterns](./../_shared/DESIGN_PATTERNS.md)

## Color System

**Every project should define:**
- Primary, Secondary, Accent
- Background, Foreground, Muted
- Destructive, Success, Warning

### Contrast Requirements
| Use Case | Minimum |
|----------|---------|
| Body text | 4.5:1 (AA) |
| Large text | 3:1 (AA) |
| UI components | 3:1 (AA) |

## Typography

### Font Stack
```css
--font-sans: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, sans-serif;
--font-mono: 'SF Mono', Monaco, 'Courier New', monospace;
```

### Type Scale
| Name | Size | Use |
|------|------|-----|
| sm | 14px | Secondary text |
| base | 16px | Body text |
| lg | 18px | Lead paragraphs |
| xl | 20px | Section headers |
| 2xl | 24px | Page headers |

## Spacing System

**Base unit:** 4px or 8px

| Token | 4px Base | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| 1 | 4px | Tight spacing |
| 2 | 8px | Default gap |
| 4 | 16px | Card padding |
| 6 | 24px | Page margins |

## Animation

### GPU-Accelerated Only
**DO animate:** `transform`, `opacity`
**AVOID:** `width`, `height`, `top`, `left`, `margin`, `padding`

### Duration
| Token | Duration | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| fast | 100ms | Micro-interactions |
| DEFAULT | 200ms | Most transitions |
| slow | 300ms | Complex animations |

### Timing
```css
--ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.68, -0.55, 0.265, 1.55);
```

## Components

### Buttons
| Variant | Use |
|---------|-----|
| Primary | Main actions |
| Secondary | Alternative actions |
| Ghost | Subtle actions |
| Destructive | Delete actions |

### Form Inputs
- Clear focus states
- Error states with messages
- Label always visible
- Required indicator

### Modals
- Focus trapped inside
- Escape to close
- Dark overlay

## Responsive Breakpoints

| Token | Min Width |
|-------|-----------|
| sm | 640px |
| md | 768px |
| lg | 1024px |
| xl | 1280px |

## Dark Mode

```css
:root { --background: #ffffff; --foreground: #000000; }
[data-theme="dark"] { --background: #000000; --foreground: #ffffff; }
```

## See Also

- [Design Patterns](./../_shared/DESIGN_PATTERNS.md) - Core patterns
- [Code Review Standards](./../_shared/CODE_REVIEW_STANDARDS.md) - Accessibility
- Project `CLAUDE.md` - Design constraints

+Design system reference adapting to any project's visual language.
-| Dark | Reduced blue light, darker backgrounds |
-/* Custom fonts loaded via @font-face or Google Fonts */

---
name: generic-design-system
description: Complete design system reference for any project - colors, typography, spacing, components, animations. Adapts to project theme and tech stack. Use when implementing UI, choosing colors, creating animations, or ensuring brand consistency.
---

# Generic Design System

Comprehensive design system reference that adapts to any project's visual language.

## Color System

### Defining Project Colors

Every project should define:
- **Primary**: Main brand color
- **Secondary**: Supporting color
- **Accent**: Highlight/CTA color
- **Background**: Base canvas color
- **Foreground**: Primary text color
- **Muted**: Secondary elements
- **Destructive**: Error/danger states
- **Success**: Positive states
- **Warning**: Caution states

### Color Mode Support

| Mode | Considerations |
|------|----------------|
| Light | Higher contrast, softer backgrounds |
| Dark | Reduced blue light, darker backgrounds |
| High Contrast | WCAG AAA compliance |

### Contrast Requirements

| Use Case | Minimum Ratio |
|----------|---------------|
| Body text | 4.5:1 (AA) |
| Large text (24px+) | 3:1 (AA) |
| UI components | 3:1 (AA) |
| Enhanced | 7:1 (AAA) |

## Typography

### Font Stack

```css
/* System fonts for performance */
--font-sans: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, sans-serif;
--font-mono: 'SF Mono', Monaco, 'Courier New', monospace;

/* Custom fonts loaded via @font-face or Google Fonts */
```

### Type Scale

| Name | Size | Line Height | Use |
|------|------|-------------|-----|
| xs | 12px | 16px | Captions, labels |
| sm | 14px | 20px | Secondary text |
| base | 16px | 24px | Body text |
| lg | 18px | 28px | Lead paragraphs |
| xl | 20px | 28px | Section headers |
| 2xl | 24px | 32px | Page headers |
| 3xl | 30px | 36px | Hero text |
| 4xl | 36px | 40px | Display |

### Font Weights

| Weight | Value | Use |
|--------|-------|-----|
| Normal | 400 | Body text |
| Medium | 500 | UI elements |
| Semibold | 600 | Subheadings |
| Bold | 700 | Headings |

## Spacing System

### Base Unit

Recommend 4px or 8px base unit for consistency.

| Token | 4px Base | 8px Base | Use |
|-------|----------|----------|-----|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | None |
| 1 | 4px | 8px | Tight spacing |
| 2 | 8px | 16px | Default gap |
| 3 | 12px | 24px | Section padding |
| 4 | 16px | 32px | Card padding |
| 5 | 20px | 40px | Section margins |
| 6 | 24px | 48px | Page margins |
| 8 | 32px | 64px | Large sections |

### Layout Grid

```css
/* Common container widths */
--container-sm: 640px;
--container-md: 768px;
--container-lg: 1024px;
--container-xl: 1280px;
--container-2xl: 1536px;
```

## Border Radius

| Token | Value | Use |
|-------|-------|-----|
| none | 0 | Sharp corners |
| sm | 2px | Subtle rounding |
| DEFAULT | 4px | Buttons, inputs |
| md | 6px | Cards |
| lg | 8px | Modals |
| xl | 12px | Large cards |
| 2xl | 16px | Hero sections |
| full | 9999px | Pills, avatars |

## Shadows

| Token | Use |
|-------|-----|
| sm | Subtle elevation (buttons) |
| DEFAULT | Standard elevation (cards) |
| md | Medium elevation (dropdowns) |
| lg | High elevation (modals) |
| xl | Maximum elevation (popovers) |

## Animation

### Timing Functions

```css
--ease-default: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 1, 1);
--ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-in-out: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
--ease-bounce: cubic-bezier(0.68, -0.55, 0.265, 1.55);
```

### Duration

| Token | Duration | Use |
|-------|----------|-----|
| fast | 100ms | Micro-interactions |
| DEFAULT | 200ms | Most transitions |
| slow | 300ms | Complex animations |
| slower | 500ms | Page transitions |

### GPU-Accelerated Properties

**DO animate:**
- `transform` (translate, scale, rotate)
- `opacity`

**AVOID animating:**
- `width`, `height`
- `top`, `left`, `right`, `bottom`
- `margin`, `padding`
- `background-color` (OK for simple)

## Components

### Buttons

| Variant | Use |
|---------|-----|
| Primary | Main actions (Submit, Save) |
| Secondary | Alternative actions |
| Ghost | Subtle actions |
| Outline | Less prominent actions |
| Destructive | Delete, remove actions |

### Form Inputs

- Clear focus states
- Error states with messages
- Placeholder text as hint
- Label always visible
- Required indicator

### Cards

- Consistent padding
- Optional header/footer
- Hover state for interactive
- Focus state for keyboard

### Modals/Dialogs

- Focus trapped inside
- Escape to close
- Click outside to close (optional)
- Centered or slide-in
- Dark overlay

## Icons

### Size Tokens

| Token | Size | Use |
|-------|------|-----|
| xs | 12px | Inline with small text |
| sm | 16px | Inline with body |
| md | 20px | Buttons, inputs |
| lg | 24px | Headers |
| xl | 32px | Features |

### Stroke Width

- 1.5px for light icons
- 2px for standard icons
- 2.5px for bold icons

## Responsive Breakpoints

| Token | Min Width | Devices |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| sm | 640px | Large phones |
| md | 768px | Tablets |
| lg | 1024px | Laptops |
| xl | 1280px | Desktops |
| 2xl | 1536px | Large desktops |

## Dark Mode Strategy

```css
/* CSS Variables approach */
:root {
  --background: #ffffff;
  --foreground: #000000;
}

[data-theme="dark"] {
  --background: #000000;
  --foreground: #ffffff;
}

/* Or media query */
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  :root {
    --background: #000000;
    --foreground: #ffffff;
  }
}
```

## Implementation Patterns

### CSS Variables (Recommended)

```css
:root {
  --color-primary: #3b82f6;
  --spacing-4: 16px;
  --radius-md: 6px;
}

.button {
  background: var(--color-primary);
  padding: var(--spacing-4);
  border-radius: var(--radius-md);
}
```

### Tailwind Config

```js
module.exports = {
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: { primary: '#3b82f6' },
      spacing: { '4': '16px' },
      borderRadius: { md: '6px' }
    }
  }
}
```

## See Also

- Project-specific design system skills for detailed brand requirements
- `.claude/workflow-checklist.md` - Styling checklist
- Project's `CLAUDE.md` for design constraints

---
name: generic-feature-developer
description: Guide feature development with architecture patterns for any tech stack. Covers frontend, backend, full-stack, and automation projects. Use when adding new features, modifying systems, or planning changes.
---

# Generic Feature Developer

Guide feature development across any tech stack.

## Development Workflow

1. **Understand** - Read CLAUDE.md, identify affected files, list constraints
2. **Plan** - Choose patterns, design data flow, identify edge cases
3. **Implement** - Small testable changes, commit frequently
4. **Test & Document** - Write tests, update docs, performance check

## Architecture by Project Type

### Static Sites
```
project/
├── index.html
├── css/           # variables.css, style.css
├── js/            # main.js, utils.js
└── assets/
```
**Patterns:** CSS variables, ES modules, event delegation

### React/Next.js
```
src/
├── components/    # ui/, features/, layout/
├── hooks/
├── stores/
├── services/
└── types/
```
**Patterns:** Container/Presentational, custom hooks, Zustand/React Query

### NestJS Backend
```
src/
├── modules/[feature]/
│   ├── feature.module.ts
│   ├── feature.controller.ts
│   ├── feature.service.ts
│   └── dto/
└── common/        # guards, decorators
```
**Patterns:** Module organization, DTOs, Guards, Prisma

## Common Features

### Adding UI Component
1. Create component → 2. Export → 3. Add tests → 4. Document

### Adding API Endpoint
1. Define route → 2. Add validation → 3. Implement service → 4. Test

### Adding State Management
1. Define shape → 2. Create store → 3. Add actions → 4. Connect components

### Database Changes
1. Design schema → 2. Create migration → 3. Update models → 4. Add service

## Error Handling

### Frontend
```typescript
try {
  await apiCall();
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof ValidationError) showFieldErrors(error.fields);
  else showGenericError();
}
```

### Backend
```typescript
if (err instanceof ValidationError) return res.status(400).json({ errors: err.errors });
if (err instanceof AuthError) return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Unauthorized' });
```

## Data Flow Patterns

**React:** User Action → Dispatch → Store → Re-render
**API:** Request → Middleware → Controller → Service → Database → Response

## Testing Strategy

| Layer | Type | Tools |
|-------|------|-------|
| UI | Component | Testing Library |
| Logic | Unit | Jest, Vitest |
| API | Integration | Supertest |
| E2E | End-to-end | Playwright |

## Performance

**Frontend:** Code splitting, lazy loading, memoization, debounce
**Backend:** DB indexing, caching, connection pooling, background jobs

## See Also

- [Code Review Standards](./../_shared/CODE_REVIEW_STANDARDS.md) - Quality
- [Design Patterns](./../_shared/DESIGN_PATTERNS.md) - UI patterns
- Project `CLAUDE.md` - Workflow rules

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: generic-feature-developer
description: Guide feature development with architecture patterns for any tech stack. Covers frontend, backend, full-stack, and automation projects. Use when adding new features, modifying systems, or planning changes.
---

# Generic Feature Developer

Guide feature development across any tech stack with proven architecture patterns.

## Feature Development Workflow

### 1. Understand Requirements
- Read existing CLAUDE.md and documentation
- Identify affected systems/files
- List dependencies and constraints
- Define acceptance criteria

### 2. Plan Architecture
- Choose appropriate patterns
- Design data flow
- Identify edge cases
- Plan error handling

### 3. Implement Incrementally
- Small, testable changes
- Commit frequently
- Update task file as you go
- Get approval at milestones

### 4. Test & Document
- Write/update tests
- Update documentation
- Run full test suite
- Performance check

## Architecture Patterns by Project Type

### Static Sites (HTML/CSS/JS)

```
project/
├── index.html          # Main entry
├── css/
│   ├── reset.css       # CSS reset
│   ├── variables.css   # CSS custom properties
│   └── style.css       # Main styles
├── js/
│   ├── main.js         # Entry point
│   └── utils.js        # Utilities
└── assets/
    ├── images/
    └── fonts/
```

**Patterns:**
- CSS custom properties for theming
- ES modules for organization
- Event delegation for dynamic content
- Progressive enhancement

### React/Next.js Applications

```
src/
├── components/
│   ├── ui/             # Base UI components
│   ├── features/       # Feature-specific
│   └── layout/         # Layout components
├── hooks/              # Custom React hooks
├── lib/                # Utilities, helpers
├── stores/             # State management
├── services/           # API calls
└── types/              # TypeScript types
```

**Patterns:**
- Container/Presentational split
- Custom hooks for logic reuse
- Zustand/Redux for global state
- React Query for server state
- Compound components for complex UI

### Node.js/Express Backend

```
src/
├── routes/             # Route handlers
├── controllers/        # Business logic
├── services/           # External services
├── models/             # Data models
├── middleware/         # Express middleware
├── utils/              # Helpers
└── config/             # Configuration
```

**Patterns:**
- MVC or service layer
- Dependency injection
- Repository pattern for data
- Error middleware
- Validation middleware

### NestJS Backend

```
src/
├── modules/
│   └── [feature]/
│       ├── feature.module.ts
│       ├── feature.controller.ts
│       ├── feature.service.ts
│       └── dto/
├── common/
│   ├── guards/
│   ├── decorators/
│   └── interceptors/
└── prisma/             # Database
```

**Patterns:**
- Module-based organization
- DTOs for validation
- Guards for auth
- Interceptors for transforms
- Prisma for type-safe DB

### Python Automation

```
automation/
├── scripts/
│   └── main.py         # Entry point
├── lib/
│   ├── validators.py   # Data validation
│   └── utils.py        # Utilities
├── data/
│   └── config.json     # Configuration
├── tests/
│   └── test_main.py
└── .github/
    └── workflows/
        └── schedule.yml
```

**Patterns:**
- Dry run mode for testing
- Environment-based config
- Structured logging
- Error handling with retry
- GitHub Actions for scheduling

## Common Feature Types

### Adding a New UI Component

1. Create component file
2. Add to exports
3. Create stories/tests
4. Use in feature
5. Document usage

### Adding an API Endpoint

1. Define route
2. Create controller/handler
3. Add validation
4. Implement service logic
5. Add tests
6. Document endpoint

### Adding State Management

1. Define state shape
2. Create store/slice
3. Add actions/mutations
4. Connect to components
5. Persist if needed

### Adding Authentication

1. Choose strategy (JWT, sessions, OAuth)
2. Implement auth service
3. Add middleware/guards
4. Protect routes
5. Handle tokens
6. Add login/logout UI

### Adding Database Feature

1. Design schema
2. Create migration
3. Update models/types
4. Add repository/service
5. Integrate with API
6. Test with fixtures

## Error Handling Patterns

### Frontend

```typescript
try {
  const result = await apiCall();
  // Handle success
} catch (error) {
  if (error instanceof NetworkError) {
    showRetryDialog();
  } else if (error instanceof ValidationError) {
    showFieldErrors(error.fields);
  } else {
    showGenericError();
    reportToSentry(error);
  }
}
```

### Backend

```typescript
// Express error middleware
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  logger.error(err);

  if (err instanceof ValidationError) {
    return res.status(400).json({ errors: err.errors });
  }
  if (err instanceof AuthError) {
    return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Unauthorized' });
  }

  res.status(500).json({ message: 'Internal server error' });
});
```

## Data Flow Patterns

### Unidirectional (React)

```
User Action → Dispatch → Store → State Change → Re-render
```

### Request/Response (API)

```
Client Request → Middleware → Controller → Service → Database
                                                       ↓
Client Response ← Serializer ← Controller ← Service ←
```

### Event-Driven

```
Event Emitted → Event Bus → Subscribers → Side Effects
```

## Integration Patterns

### Third-Party APIs

1. Create service wrapper
2. Handle authentication
3. Implement retry logic
4. Cache responses
5. Handle rate limits
6. Mock for tests

### Database Operations

1. Use transactions for multi-step
2. Handle constraints/conflicts
3. Implement soft delete
4. Add audit logging
5. Optimize queries

## Testing Strategy

| Layer | Test Type | Tools |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| UI | Component | Testing Library |
| Logic | Unit | Jest, Vitest |
| API | Integration | Supertest |
| E2E | End-to-end | Playwright, Cypress |
| Visual | Snapshot | Storybook, Percy |

## Performance Considerations

### Frontend
- Code splitting
- Lazy loading
- Memoization
- Virtual scrolling
- Debounce/throttle

### Backend
- Database indexing
- Query optimization
- Caching (Redis)
- Connection pooling
- Background jobs

## See Also

- Project-specific feature developer skills
- `CLAUDE.md` - Project workflow rules
- `.claude/automation-guide.md` - Development workflow

1→---
     2→name: tech-debt-analyzer
     3→description: This skill should be used when analyzing technical debt in a codebase, documenting code quality issues, creating technical debt registers, or assessing code maintainability. Use this for identifying code smells, architectural issues, dependency problems, missing documentation, security vulnerabilities, and creating comprehensive technical debt documentation.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Technical Debt Analyzer
     7→
     8→## Neuman Brain CLAUDE.md Compliance
     9→
    10→**When analyzing Neuman Brain codebase, check for CLAUDE.md workflow violations:**
    11→
    12→### Workflow Debt (High Priority)
    13→- ❌ **Missing task files:** Code changes without corresponding `tasks/*.md` file
    14→- ❌ **Incomplete review sections:** Task files without comprehensive review
    15→- ❌ **Missing Created/Completed dates:** Tasks missing timestamps
    16→- ❌ **Unarchived completed tasks:** COMPLETE tasks still in `tasks/` (should be in `.archive/completed-tasks/`)
    17→- ❌ **Generic task names:** `todo.md`, `task1.md` instead of descriptive names
    18→
    19→### Code Quality Debt (CLAUDE.md Rules)
    20→- ❌ **Root Cause Not Fixed (Rule #5):** Temporary fixes, band-aids instead of proper solutions
    21→- ❌ **Surgical Simplicity Violated (Rule #10):** Changes impact too much code, refactors without reason
    22→- ❌ **Missing Documentation (Rule #11):** New features without updating `docs/`
    23→- ❌ **Performance Debt:** Bundle size > 100KB gzipped, non-GPU-accelerated animations
    24→- ❌ **Security Debt:** `any` types, innerHTML with user content, no input sanitization
    25→- ❌ **Accessibility Debt:** Missing ARIA labels, no keyboard nav, < 4.5:1 contrast
    26→
    27→### Neuman Brain-Specific Checks
    28→```typescript
    29→// ❌ DEBT: Not following privacy-first principle
    30→localStorage.setItem('userData', data); // Should use IndexedDB
    31→
    32→// ❌ DEBT: Not following performance requirements
    33→.animate { margin-top: -4px; } // Should use transform
    34→
    35→// ❌ DEBT: Not following type safety (Rule #19)
    36→function process(data: any) { } // Should use unknown + validation
    37→
    38→// ❌ DEBT: Not following design system
    39→<div style={{color: '#FF5733'}}> // Should use design system colors
    40→```
    41→
    42→### See Also
    43→- `CLAUDE.md` - Complete workflow and development principles
    44→- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-design-system/` - Design system requirements
    45→- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Privacy/security/UX/performance
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## Overview
    50→
    51→Systematically identify, analyze, document, and track technical debt in JavaScript/TypeScript codebases. This skill provides automated analysis tools, comprehensive debt categorization frameworks, and documentation templates to maintain a technical debt register.
    52→
    53→## Core Workflow
    54→
    55→### 1. Automated Analysis
    56→
    57→Run automated scripts to detect technical debt indicators across the codebase.
    58→
    59→#### Code Smell Detection
    60→
    61→Identify code quality issues using the automated detector:
    62→
    63→```bash
    64→python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py src --output markdown
    65→```
    66→
    67→The script analyzes:
    68→- **Large Files:** Files exceeding 500 lines
    69→- **Complex Functions:** High cyclomatic complexity (>10) or long functions (>50 lines)
    70→- **Debt Markers:** TODO, FIXME, HACK, XXX, BUG comments
    71→- **Console Statements:** Debug statements left in code
    72→- **Weak Typing:** Use of `any` type in TypeScript
    73→- **Long Parameters:** Functions with >5 parameters
    74→- **Deep Nesting:** Code nested >4 levels deep
    75→- **Magic Numbers:** Hardcoded numeric values
    76→
    77→**Output Example:**
    78→```
    79→# Technical Debt Analysis Report
    80→
    81→**Files Analyzed:** 127
    82→**Total Lines:** 15,432
    83→**Total Issues:** 89
    84→
    85→### Issues by Severity
    86→- HIGH: 23
    87→- MEDIUM: 41
    88→- LOW: 25
    89→
    90→## Large Files (12 issues)
    91→### High Priority
    92→- src/components/Dashboard.tsx (847 lines): File too large
    93→- src/services/DataProcessor.ts (623 lines): File too large
    94→...
    95→```
    96→
    97→#### Dependency Analysis
    98→
    99→Examine dependencies for debt indicators:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py package.json
   103→```
   104→
   105→The script identifies:
   106→- **Deprecated Packages:** Known deprecated libraries (request, tslint, etc.)
   107→- **Duplicate Functionality:** Multiple packages serving same purpose
   108→- **Version Issues:** Overly loose or strict version constraints
   109→- **Security Concerns:** Known vulnerable packages (requires audit data)
   110→
   111→**Output Example:**
   112→```
   113→# Dependency Analysis Report
   114→
   115→**Package:** expense-tracker
   116→**Dependencies:** 24
   117→**Dev Dependencies:** 18
   118→**Total Issues:** 7
   119→
   120→## Deprecated/Outdated Packages (3)
   121→### request [HIGH]
   122→Using deprecated package - use axios, node-fetch, or got instead
   123→- Current version: ^2.88.0
   124→
   125→## Duplicate Functionality (2)
   126→### HTTP client [MEDIUM]
   127→Multiple packages for HTTP client: axios, node-fetch
   128→```
   129→
   130→### 2. Manual Code Review
   131→
   132→Complement automated analysis with manual review for issues that require human judgment.
   133→
   134→#### Review Focus Areas
   135→
   136→**Architectural Debt:**
   137→- Tight coupling between components
   138→- Missing abstractions
   139→- Poor separation of concerns
   140→- Circular dependencies
   141→
   142→**Test Debt:**
   143→- Missing test coverage for critical paths
   144→- Fragile tests coupled to implementation
   145→- No integration or E2E tests
   146→- Slow test execution
   147→
   148→**Documentation Debt:**
   149→- Missing README or setup instructions
   150→- No architecture documentation
   151→- Outdated API docs
   152→- Missing ADRs for major decisions
   153→
   154→**Performance Debt:**
   155→- N+1 query problems
   156→- Inefficient algorithms
   157→- Memory leaks
   158→- Large bundle sizes
   159→
   160→**Security Debt:**
   161→- Missing input validation
   162→- No authentication/authorization
   163→- SQL injection vulnerabilities
   164→- XSS vulnerabilities
   165→- Exposed secrets
   166→
   167→### 3. Categorize and Assess
   168→
   169→Organize findings using the standardized debt categories.
   170→
   171→#### Debt Categories
   172→
   173→Refer to `references/debt_categories.md` for comprehensive details on:
   174→
   175→1. **Code Quality Debt:** Code smells, complexity, duplication
   176→2. **Architectural Debt:** Structure, coupling, abstractions
   177→3. **Test Debt:** Coverage gaps, fragile tests
   178→4. **Documentation Debt:** Missing or outdated docs
   179→5. **Dependency Debt:** Outdated or problematic dependencies
   180→6. **Performance Debt:** Inefficiencies and bottlenecks
   181→7. **Security Debt:** Vulnerabilities and weaknesses
   182→8. **Infrastructure Debt:** DevOps and deployment issues
   183→9. **Design Debt:** UI/UX inconsistencies
   184→
   185→#### Severity Assessment
   186→
   187→Assign severity based on impact and urgency:
   188→
   189→**Critical:**
   190→- Security vulnerabilities
   191→- Production-breaking issues
   192→- Data loss risks
   193→- **Action:** Immediate fix required
   194→
   195→**High:**
   196→- Significant performance problems
   197→- Architectural issues blocking features
   198→- High-risk untested code
   199→- **Action:** Fix within current/next sprint
   200→
   201→**Medium:**
   202→- Code quality issues in frequently changed files
   203→- Missing documentation
   204→- Outdated dependencies (non-security)
   205→- **Action:** Address within quarter
   206→
   207→**Low:**
   208→- Minor code smells
   209→- Optimization opportunities
   210→- Nice-to-have improvements
   211→- **Action:** Address when convenient
   212→
   213→#### Priority Matrix
   214→
   215→| Impact / Effort | Low Effort | Medium Effort | High Effort |
   216→|----------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
   217→| High Impact    | Do First  | Do Second     | Plan & Do   |
   218→| Medium Impact  | Do Second | Plan & Do     | Consider    |
   219→| Low Impact     | Quick Win | Consider      | Avoid       |
   220→
   221→### 4. Document Findings
   222→
   223→Create comprehensive documentation of technical debt.
   224→
   225→#### Technical Debt Register
   226→
   227→Use the provided template to maintain a debt register:
   228→
   229→**Template Location:** `assets/DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md`
   230→
   231→**Structure:**
   232→```markdown
   233→## DEBT-001: Complex UserService with 847 lines
   234→
   235→**Category:** Code Quality
   236→**Severity:** High
   237→**Location:** src/services/UserService.ts
   238→
   239→**Description:**
   240→UserService has grown to 847 lines with multiple responsibilities
   241→including authentication, profile management, and notification handling.
   242→
   243→**Impact:**
   244→- Business: Slows down feature development by 30%
   245→- Technical: Difficult to test, high bug rate
   246→- Risk: Changes frequently break unrelated functionality
   247→
   248→**Proposed Solution:**
   249→Split into separate services:
   250→- AuthenticationService
   251→- UserProfileService
   252→- NotificationService
   253→
   254→**Effort Estimate:** 3 days
   255→**Priority Justification:** High churn area blocking new features
   256→**Target Resolution:** Sprint 24
   257→```
   258→
   259→**Register Sections:**
   260→1. **Active Debt Items:** Current technical debt needing attention
   261→2. **Resolved Items:** Historical record of fixed debt
   262→3. **Won't Fix Items:** Debt accepted as acceptable trade-off
   263→4. **Trends:** Analysis by category, severity, and age
   264→5. **Review Schedule:** Regular maintenance plan
   265→
   266→#### Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
   267→
   268→Document major technical decisions using ADRs to prevent future debt.
   269→
   270→**Template Location:** `assets/ADR_TEMPLATE.md`
   271→
   272→**When to Create ADRs:**
   273→- Choosing frameworks or libraries
   274→- Architectural changes
   275→- Major refactoring decisions
   276→- Technology migrations
   277→- Performance optimization strategies
   278→
   279→**Example:**
   280→```markdown
   281→# ADR-003: Migrate from Moment.js to date-fns
   282→
   283→**Status:** Accepted
   284→**Date:** 2024-01-15
   285→
   286→## Context
   287→Moment.js is deprecated and increases bundle size by 67KB.
   288→Team needs a modern date library with tree-shaking support.
   289→
   290→## Decision
   291→Migrate to date-fns for date manipulation.
   292→
   293→## Consequences
   294→- Positive: Reduce bundle by 60KB, modern API, active maintenance
   295→- Negative: Migration effort, learning curve for team
   296→- Technical Debt: None - this resolves existing dependency debt
   297→```
   298→
   299→### 5. Prioritize and Plan
   300→
   301→Create actionable plans to address technical debt.
   302→
   303→#### Prioritization Approach
   304→
   305→1. **Critical Items:** Add to current sprint immediately
   306→2. **High Items:** Include in sprint planning
   307→3. **Medium Items:** Add to quarterly roadmap
   308→4. **Low Items:** Opportunistic fixes during related work
   309→
   310→#### Time Allocation
   311→
   312→**Recommended Allocation:**
   313→- 20% of sprint capacity for technical debt
   314→- Alternating sprints: feature sprint / debt sprint
   315→- Dedicated quarterly "tech health" sprint
   316→
   317→#### Tracking Progress
   318→
   319→Monitor debt reduction over time:
   320→
   321→**Metrics to Track:**
   322→- Total debt items (trend down)
   323→- Debt by severity (critical should be 0)
   324→- Debt age (old debt is concerning)
   325→- Resolution rate (items fixed per sprint)
   326→- New debt rate (items added per sprint)
   327→
   328→### 6. Prevention Strategies
   329→
   330→Implement practices to minimize new technical debt.
   331→
   332→#### Code Review Checklist
   333→
   334→Before approving PRs, verify:
   335→- [ ] No code smells introduced (complexity, size, nesting)
   336→- [ ] Tests added/updated with adequate coverage
   337→- [ ] Documentation updated (README, comments, ADRs)
   338→- [ ] No security vulnerabilities
   339→- [ ] Performance impact considered
   340→- [ ] No new dependencies without justification
   341→- [ ] Follows team conventions and patterns
   342→
   343→#### Automated Prevention
   344→
   345→**Linting and Formatting:**
   346→```json
   347→{
   348→  "rules": {
   349→    "complexity": ["error", 10],
   350→    "max-lines-per-function": ["error", 50],
   351→    "max-params": ["error", 5],
   352→    "max-depth": ["error", 4],
   353→    "no-console": "warn"
   354→  }
   355→}
   356→```
   357→
   358→**Required Checks:**
   359→- TypeScript strict mode enabled
   360→- Minimum test coverage threshold (80%)
   361→- No high-severity security vulnerabilities
   362→- Bundle size limits enforced
   363→
   364→#### Regular Maintenance
   365→
   366→**Weekly:**
   367→- Review and triage TODO/FIXME comments
   368→- Update debt register with new findings
   369→
   370→**Monthly:**
   371→- Dependency updates (security patches)
   372→- Debt register review
   373→- Plan fixes for high-priority items
   374→
   375→**Quarterly:**
   376→- Full codebase debt analysis
   377→- Architecture review
   378→- Major dependency updates
   379→- Trend analysis and strategy adjustment
   380→
   381→## Decision Tree
   382→
   383→Follow this workflow based on the situation:
   384→
   385→**Starting a new analysis?**
   386→→ Run automated scripts (detect_code_smells.py, analyze_dependencies.py)
   387→→ Review output for high-severity issues
   388→→ Conduct manual review for areas scripts can't detect
   389→→ Go to documentation step
   390→
   391→**Documenting findings?**
   392→→ Copy DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md to project root
   393→→ Add each debt item with full details
   394→→ Categorize by type and assign severity
   395→→ Estimate effort and prioritize
   396→→ Go to planning step
   397→
   398→**Planning debt reduction?**
   399→→ Sort by priority matrix (impact/effort)
   400→→ Allocate sprint capacity (20% recommended)
   401→→ Create tickets for top priority items
   402→→ Schedule regular reviews
   403→
   404→**Making architectural decisions?**
   405→→ Copy ADR_TEMPLATE.md
   406→→ Document context, options, and decision
   407→→ Identify any debt being incurred
   408→→ Add to debt register if applicable
   409→
   410→**Preventing new debt?**
   411→→ Implement code review checklist
   412→→ Configure automated linting/testing
   413→→ Set up regular maintenance schedule
   414→→ Monitor metrics over time
   415→
   416→## Tools and Scripts
   417→
   418→### detect_code_smells.py
   419→
   420→**Purpose:** Automated code quality analysis
   421→
   422→**Usage:**
   423→```bash
   424→python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py [src-dir] [--output json|markdown]
   425→```
   426→
   427→**Detects:**
   428→- Large files (>500 lines)
   429→- Complex functions (complexity >10)
   430→- Technical debt markers (TODO, FIXME, HACK)
   431→- Console statements
   432→- Weak TypeScript typing
   433→- Long parameter lists (>5 params)
   434→- Deep nesting (>4 levels)
   435→- Magic numbers
   436→
   437→**Output:** Markdown report or JSON for programmatic processing
   438→
   439→### analyze_dependencies.py
   440→
   441→**Purpose:** Dependency health analysis
   442→
   443→**Usage:**
   444→```bash
   445→python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py [package.json-path]
   446→```
   447→
   448→**Detects:**
   449→- Deprecated packages (request, tslint, node-sass, etc.)
   450→- Duplicate functionality (multiple date libs, http clients, etc.)
   451→- Unsafe version constraints (*, latest)
   452→- Overly strict versions (exact versions without ^ or ~)
   453→
   454→**Output:** Markdown report with recommendations
   455→
   456→## Reference Documentation
   457→
   458→### debt_categories.md
   459→
   460→Comprehensive guide to technical debt types with:
   461→- 9 major debt categories
   462→- Indicators and examples for each
   463→- Impact assessment criteria
   464→- Severity level definitions
   465→- Measurement metrics
   466→- Prevention strategies
   467→
   468→**Load this reference when:**
   469→- Need detailed examples of specific debt types
   470→- Assessing severity and impact
   471→- Understanding root causes
   472→- Planning prevention strategies
   473→
   474→## Documentation Templates
   475→
   476→### DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md
   477→
   478→Complete technical debt register template including:
   479→- Debt item structure
   480→- Status tracking
   481→- Impact assessment format
   482→- Trend analysis sections
   483→- Review schedule
   484→
   485→**Use this template to:**
   486→- Start a new debt register
   487→- Standardize debt documentation
   488→- Track debt across team/project
   489→
   490→### ADR_TEMPLATE.md
   491→
   492→Architecture Decision Record template including:
   493→- Context and problem statement
   494→- Options considered
   495→- Decision rationale
   496→- Consequences (positive and negative)
   497→- Implementation plan
   498→
   499→**Use this template to:**
   500→- Document major technical decisions
   501→- Prevent future "why did we do this?" questions
   502→- Track technical debt incurred by decisions
   503→
   504→## Best Practices
   505→
   506→### Analysis Best Practices
   507→
   508→1. **Run analysis regularly** (weekly or bi-weekly)
   509→2. **Combine automated + manual review** for comprehensive coverage
   510→3. **Focus on high-churn areas** first for maximum impact
   511→4. **Involve the team** in debt identification
   512→5. **Be objective** - all codebases have debt
   513→
   514→### Documentation Best Practices
   515→
   516→1. **Be specific** - include file names, line numbers, examples
   517→2. **Explain impact** - why does this matter?
   518→3. **Propose solutions** - don't just complain, suggest fixes
   519→4. **Estimate effort** - helps with prioritization
   520→5. **Track trends** - is debt increasing or decreasing?
   521→
   522→### Remediation Best Practices
   523→
   524→1. **Fix critical items immediately** - especially security
   525→2. **Allocate consistent time** - 20% of sprint capacity
   526→3. **Celebrate wins** - track and recognize debt reduction
   527→4. **Don't let perfect be the enemy of good** - incremental improvement
   528→5. **Prevent new debt** - easier than fixing old debt
   529→
   530→### Communication Best Practices
   531→
   532→1. **Make debt visible** - share metrics with stakeholders
   533→2. **Educate on impact** - connect debt to business outcomes
   534→3. **Get buy-in** - explain ROI of debt reduction
   535→4. **Regular updates** - include in sprint reviews
   536→5. **Avoid blame** - focus on improvement, not fault
   537→
   538→## Example Workflow
   539→
   540→Complete workflow from analysis to resolution:
   541→
   542→**Week 1: Analysis**
   543→```bash
   544→# Run automated analysis
   545→python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py src --output markdown > debt_analysis.md
   546→python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py package.json >> debt_analysis.md
   547→
   548→# Manual review of critical areas
   549→# - Authentication logic
   550→# - Payment processing
   551→# - Data models
   552→```
   553→
   554→**Week 1-2: Documentation**
   555→```bash
   556→# Create debt register from template
   557→cp assets/DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md TECHNICAL_DEBT.md
   558→
   559→# Add findings to register with:
   560→# - Category and severity
   561→# - Impact assessment
   562→# - Effort estimation
   563→# - Priority assignment
   564→```
   565→
   566→**Week 2: Prioritization**
   567→```
   568→# Team review session
   569→# - Review all high/critical items
   570→# - Discuss quick wins (high impact, low effort)
   571→# - Allocate sprint capacity
   572→# - Create tickets for top 5 items
   573→```
   574→
   575→**Weeks 3-6: Remediation**
   576→```
   577→# Sprint work
   578→# - Fix 2-3 debt items per sprint
   579→# - Update debt register as items resolved
   580→# - Create ADRs for major refactoring decisions
   581→# - Monitor metrics
   582→```
   583→
   584→**Monthly: Review**
   585→```
   586→# Trend analysis
   587→# - Total debt (should decrease)
   588→# - New debt rate (should be low)
   589→# - Age of oldest items (should decrease)
   590→# - Categories most affected
   591→
   592→# Adjust strategy based on trends
   593→```
   594→
   595→---
   596→
   597→## Success Metrics
   598→
   599→Track these metrics to measure debt reduction effectiveness:
   600→
   601→**Quantity Metrics:**
   602→- Total debt items (trending down)
   603→- Debt by severity (zero critical)
   604→- Debt items per 1000 LOC
   605→
   606→**Quality Metrics:**
   607→- Test coverage (trending up)
   608→- Cyclomatic complexity (trending down)
   609→- Average file/function size (stable or decreasing)
   610→
   611→**Velocity Metrics:**
   612→- Debt items resolved per sprint
   613→- New debt items per sprint (should be low)
   614→- Time to resolve (should decrease)
   615→
   616→**Business Metrics:**
   617→- Bug rate (should decrease)
   618→- Feature delivery speed (should increase)
   619→- Developer satisfaction (should increase)
   620→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
---
name: tech-debt-analyzer
description: This skill should be used when analyzing technical debt in a codebase, documenting code quality issues, creating technical debt registers, or assessing code maintainability. Use this for identifying code smells, architectural issues, dependency problems, missing documentation, security vulnerabilities, and creating comprehensive technical debt documentation.
---

# Technical Debt Analyzer

## Neuman Brain CLAUDE.md Compliance

**When analyzing Neuman Brain codebase, check for CLAUDE.md workflow violations:**

### Workflow Debt (High Priority)
- ❌ **Missing task files:** Code changes without corresponding `tasks/*.md` file
- ❌ **Incomplete review sections:** Task files without comprehensive review
- ❌ **Missing Created/Completed dates:** Tasks missing timestamps
- ❌ **Unarchived completed tasks:** COMPLETE tasks still in `tasks/` (should be in `.archive/completed-tasks/`)
- ❌ **Generic task names:** `todo.md`, `task1.md` instead of descriptive names

### Code Quality Debt (CLAUDE.md Rules)
- ❌ **Root Cause Not Fixed (Rule #5):** Temporary fixes, band-aids instead of proper solutions
- ❌ **Surgical Simplicity Violated (Rule #10):** Changes impact too much code, refactors without reason
- ❌ **Missing Documentation (Rule #11):** New features without updating `docs/`
- ❌ **Performance Debt:** Bundle size > 100KB gzipped, non-GPU-accelerated animations
- ❌ **Security Debt:** `any` types, innerHTML with user content, no input sanitization
- ❌ **Accessibility Debt:** Missing ARIA labels, no keyboard nav, < 4.5:1 contrast

### Neuman Brain-Specific Checks
```typescript
// ❌ DEBT: Not following privacy-first principle
localStorage.setItem('userData', data); // Should use IndexedDB

// ❌ DEBT: Not following performance requirements
.animate { margin-top: -4px; } // Should use transform

// ❌ DEBT: Not following type safety (Rule #19)
function process(data: any) { } // Should use unknown + validation

// ❌ DEBT: Not following design system
<div style={{color: '#FF5733'}}> // Should use design system colors
```

### See Also
- `CLAUDE.md` - Complete workflow and development principles
- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-design-system/` - Design system requirements
- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Privacy/security/UX/performance

---

## Overview

Systematically identify, analyze, document, and track technical debt in JavaScript/TypeScript codebases. This skill provides automated analysis tools, comprehensive debt categorization frameworks, and documentation templates to maintain a technical debt register.

## Core Workflow

### 1. Automated Analysis

Run automated scripts to detect technical debt indicators across the codebase.

#### Code Smell Detection

Identify code quality issues using the automated detector:

```bash
python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py src --output markdown
```

The script analyzes:
- **Large Files:** Files exceeding 500 lines
- **Complex Functions:** High cyclomatic complexity (>10) or long functions (>50 lines)
- **Debt Markers:** TODO, FIXME, HACK, XXX, BUG comments
- **Console Statements:** Debug statements left in code
- **Weak Typing:** Use of `any` type in TypeScript
- **Long Parameters:** Functions with >5 parameters
- **Deep Nesting:** Code nested >4 levels deep
- **Magic Numbers:** Hardcoded numeric values

**Output Example:**
```
# Technical Debt Analysis Report

**Files Analyzed:** 127
**Total Lines:** 15,432
**Total Issues:** 89

### Issues by Severity
- HIGH: 23
- MEDIUM: 41
- LOW: 25

## Large Files (12 issues)
### High Priority
- src/components/Dashboard.tsx (847 lines): File too large
- src/services/DataProcessor.ts (623 lines): File too large
...
```

#### Dependency Analysis

Examine dependencies for debt indicators:

```bash
python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py package.json
```

The script identifies:
- **Deprecated Packages:** Known deprecated libraries (request, tslint, etc.)
- **Duplicate Functionality:** Multiple packages serving same purpose
- **Version Issues:** Overly loose or strict version constraints
- **Security Concerns:** Known vulnerable packages (requires audit data)

**Output Example:**
```
# Dependency Analysis Report

**Package:** expense-tracker
**Dependencies:** 24
**Dev Dependencies:** 18
**Total Issues:** 7

## Deprecated/Outdated Packages (3)
### request [HIGH]
Using deprecated package - use axios, node-fetch, or got instead
- Current version: ^2.88.0

## Duplicate Functionality (2)
### HTTP client [MEDIUM]
Multiple packages for HTTP client: axios, node-fetch
```

### 2. Manual Code Review

Complement automated analysis with manual review for issues that require human judgment.

#### Review Focus Areas

**Architectural Debt:**
- Tight coupling between components
- Missing abstractions
- Poor separation of concerns
- Circular dependencies

**Test Debt:**
- Missing test coverage for critical paths
- Fragile tests coupled to implementation
- No integration or E2E tests
- Slow test execution

**Documentation Debt:**
- Missing README or setup instructions
- No architecture documentation
- Outdated API docs
- Missing ADRs for major decisions

**Performance Debt:**
- N+1 query problems
- Inefficient algorithms
- Memory leaks
- Large bundle sizes

**Security Debt:**
- Missing input validation
- No authentication/authorization
- SQL injection vulnerabilities
- XSS vulnerabilities
- Exposed secrets

### 3. Categorize and Assess

Organize findings using the standardized debt categories.

#### Debt Categories

Refer to `references/debt_categories.md` for comprehensive details on:

1. **Code Quality Debt:** Code smells, complexity, duplication
2. **Architectural Debt:** Structure, coupling, abstractions
3. **Test Debt:** Coverage gaps, fragile tests
4. **Documentation Debt:** Missing or outdated docs
5. **Dependency Debt:** Outdated or problematic dependencies
6. **Performance Debt:** Inefficiencies and bottlenecks
7. **Security Debt:** Vulnerabilities and weaknesses
8. **Infrastructure Debt:** DevOps and deployment issues
9. **Design Debt:** UI/UX inconsistencies

#### Severity Assessment

Assign severity based on impact and urgency:

**Critical:**
- Security vulnerabilities
- Production-breaking issues
- Data loss risks
- **Action:** Immediate fix required

**High:**
- Significant performance problems
- Architectural issues blocking features
- High-risk untested code
- **Action:** Fix within current/next sprint

**Medium:**
- Code quality issues in frequently changed files
- Missing documentation
- Outdated dependencies (non-security)
- **Action:** Address within quarter

**Low:**
- Minor code smells
- Optimization opportunities
- Nice-to-have improvements
- **Action:** Address when convenient

#### Priority Matrix

| Impact / Effort | Low Effort | Medium Effort | High Effort |
|----------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| High Impact    | Do First  | Do Second     | Plan & Do   |
| Medium Impact  | Do Second | Plan & Do     | Consider    |
| Low Impact     | Quick Win | Consider      | Avoid       |

### 4. Document Findings

Create comprehensive documentation of technical debt.

#### Technical Debt Register

Use the provided template to maintain a debt register:

**Template Location:** `assets/DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md`

**Structure:**
```markdown
## DEBT-001: Complex UserService with 847 lines

**Category:** Code Quality
**Severity:** High
**Location:** src/services/UserService.ts

**Description:**
UserService has grown to 847 lines with multiple responsibilities
including authentication, profile management, and notification handling.

**Impact:**
- Business: Slows down feature development by 30%
- Technical: Difficult to test, high bug rate
- Risk: Changes frequently break unrelated functionality

**Proposed Solution:**
Split into separate services:
- AuthenticationService
- UserProfileService
- NotificationService

**Effort Estimate:** 3 days
**Priority Justification:** High churn area blocking new features
**Target Resolution:** Sprint 24
```

**Register Sections:**
1. **Active Debt Items:** Current technical debt needing attention
2. **Resolved Items:** Historical record of fixed debt
3. **Won't Fix Items:** Debt accepted as acceptable trade-off
4. **Trends:** Analysis by category, severity, and age
5. **Review Schedule:** Regular maintenance plan

#### Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

Document major technical decisions using ADRs to prevent future debt.

**Template Location:** `assets/ADR_TEMPLATE.md`

**When to Create ADRs:**
- Choosing frameworks or libraries
- Architectural changes
- Major refactoring decisions
- Technology migrations
- Performance optimization strategies

**Example:**
```markdown
# ADR-003: Migrate from Moment.js to date-fns

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2024-01-15

## Context
Moment.js is deprecated and increases bundle size by 67KB.
Team needs a modern date library with tree-shaking support.

## Decision
Migrate to date-fns for date manipulation.

## Consequences
- Positive: Reduce bundle by 60KB, modern API, active maintenance
- Negative: Migration effort, learning curve for team
- Technical Debt: None - this resolves existing dependency debt
```

### 5. Prioritize and Plan

Create actionable plans to address technical debt.

#### Prioritization Approach

1. **Critical Items:** Add to current sprint immediately
2. **High Items:** Include in sprint planning
3. **Medium Items:** Add to quarterly roadmap
4. **Low Items:** Opportunistic fixes during related work

#### Time Allocation

**Recommended Allocation:**
- 20% of sprint capacity for technical debt
- Alternating sprints: feature sprint / debt sprint
- Dedicated quarterly "tech health" sprint

#### Tracking Progress

Monitor debt reduction over time:

**Metrics to Track:**
- Total debt items (trend down)
- Debt by severity (critical should be 0)
- Debt age (old debt is concerning)
- Resolution rate (items fixed per sprint)
- New debt rate (items added per sprint)

### 6. Prevention Strategies

Implement practices to minimize new technical debt.

#### Code Review Checklist

Before approving PRs, verify:
- [ ] No code smells introduced (complexity, size, nesting)
- [ ] Tests added/updated with adequate coverage
- [ ] Documentation updated (README, comments, ADRs)
- [ ] No security vulnerabilities
- [ ] Performance impact considered
- [ ] No new dependencies without justification
- [ ] Follows team conventions and patterns

#### Automated Prevention

**Linting and Formatting:**
```json
{
  "rules": {
    "complexity": ["error", 10],
    "max-lines-per-function": ["error", 50],
    "max-params": ["error", 5],
    "max-depth": ["error", 4],
    "no-console": "warn"
  }
}
```

**Required Checks:**
- TypeScript strict mode enabled
- Minimum test coverage threshold (80%)
- No high-severity security vulnerabilities
- Bundle size limits enforced

#### Regular Maintenance

**Weekly:**
- Review and triage TODO/FIXME comments
- Update debt register with new findings

**Monthly:**
- Dependency updates (security patches)
- Debt register review
- Plan fixes for high-priority items

**Quarterly:**
- Full codebase debt analysis
- Architecture review
- Major dependency updates
- Trend analysis and strategy adjustment

## Decision Tree

Follow this workflow based on the situation:

**Starting a new analysis?**
→ Run automated scripts (detect_code_smells.py, analyze_dependencies.py)
→ Review output for high-severity issues
→ Conduct manual review for areas scripts can't detect
→ Go to documentation step

**Documenting findings?**
→ Copy DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md to project root
→ Add each debt item with full details
→ Categorize by type and assign severity
→ Estimate effort and prioritize
→ Go to planning step

**Planning debt reduction?**
→ Sort by priority matrix (impact/effort)
→ Allocate sprint capacity (20% recommended)
→ Create tickets for top priority items
→ Schedule regular reviews

**Making architectural decisions?**
→ Copy ADR_TEMPLATE.md
→ Document context, options, and decision
→ Identify any debt being incurred
→ Add to debt register if applicable

**Preventing new debt?**
→ Implement code review checklist
→ Configure automated linting/testing
→ Set up regular maintenance schedule
→ Monitor metrics over time

## Tools and Scripts

### detect_code_smells.py

**Purpose:** Automated code quality analysis

**Usage:**
```bash
python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py [src-dir] [--output json|markdown]
```

**Detects:**
- Large files (>500 lines)
- Complex functions (complexity >10)
- Technical debt markers (TODO, FIXME, HACK)
- Console statements
- Weak TypeScript typing
- Long parameter lists (>5 params)
- Deep nesting (>4 levels)
- Magic numbers

**Output:** Markdown report or JSON for programmatic processing

### analyze_dependencies.py

**Purpose:** Dependency health analysis

**Usage:**
```bash
python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py [package.json-path]
```

**Detects:**
- Deprecated packages (request, tslint, node-sass, etc.)
- Duplicate functionality (multiple date libs, http clients, etc.)
- Unsafe version constraints (*, latest)
- Overly strict versions (exact versions without ^ or ~)

**Output:** Markdown report with recommendations

## Reference Documentation

### debt_categories.md

Comprehensive guide to technical debt types with:
- 9 major debt categories
- Indicators and examples for each
- Impact assessment criteria
- Severity level definitions
- Measurement metrics
- Prevention strategies

**Load this reference when:**
- Need detailed examples of specific debt types
- Assessing severity and impact
- Understanding root causes
- Planning prevention strategies

## Documentation Templates

### DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md

Complete technical debt register template including:
- Debt item structure
- Status tracking
- Impact assessment format
- Trend analysis sections
- Review schedule

**Use this template to:**
- Start a new debt register
- Standardize debt documentation
- Track debt across team/project

### ADR_TEMPLATE.md

Architecture Decision Record template including:
- Context and problem statement
- Options considered
- Decision rationale
- Consequences (positive and negative)
- Implementation plan

**Use this template to:**
- Document major technical decisions
- Prevent future "why did we do this?" questions
- Track technical debt incurred by decisions

## Best Practices

### Analysis Best Practices

1. **Run analysis regularly** (weekly or bi-weekly)
2. **Combine automated + manual review** for comprehensive coverage
3. **Focus on high-churn areas** first for maximum impact
4. **Involve the team** in debt identification
5. **Be objective** - all codebases have debt

### Documentation Best Practices

1. **Be specific** - include file names, line numbers, examples
2. **Explain impact** - why does this matter?
3. **Propose solutions** - don't just complain, suggest fixes
4. **Estimate effort** - helps with prioritization
5. **Track trends** - is debt increasing or decreasing?

### Remediation Best Practices

1. **Fix critical items immediately** - especially security
2. **Allocate consistent time** - 20% of sprint capacity
3. **Celebrate wins** - track and recognize debt reduction
4. **Don't let perfect be the enemy of good** - incremental improvement
5. **Prevent new debt** - easier than fixing old debt

### Communication Best Practices

1. **Make debt visible** - share metrics with stakeholders
2. **Educate on impact** - connect debt to business outcomes
3. **Get buy-in** - explain ROI of debt reduction
4. **Regular updates** - include in sprint reviews
5. **Avoid blame** - focus on improvement, not fault

## Example Workflow

Complete workflow from analysis to resolution:

**Week 1: Analysis**
```bash
# Run automated analysis
python3 scripts/detect_code_smells.py src --output markdown > debt_analysis.md
python3 scripts/analyze_dependencies.py package.json >> debt_analysis.md

# Manual review of critical areas
# - Authentication logic
# - Payment processing
# - Data models
```

**Week 1-2: Documentation**
```bash
# Create debt register from template
cp assets/DEBT_REGISTER_TEMPLATE.md TECHNICAL_DEBT.md

# Add findings to register with:
# - Category and severity
# - Impact assessment
# - Effort estimation
# - Priority assignment
```

**Week 2: Prioritization**
```
# Team review session
# - Review all high/critical items
# - Discuss quick wins (high impact, low effort)
# - Allocate sprint capacity
# - Create tickets for top 5 items
```

**Weeks 3-6: Remediation**
```
# Sprint work
# - Fix 2-3 debt items per sprint
# - Update debt register as items resolved
# - Create ADRs for major refactoring decisions
# - Monitor metrics
```

**Monthly: Review**
```
# Trend analysis
# - Total debt (should decrease)
# - New debt rate (should be low)
# - Age of oldest items (should decrease)
# - Categories most affected

# Adjust strategy based on trends
```

---

## Success Metrics

Track these metrics to measure debt reduction effectiveness:

**Quantity Metrics:**
- Total debt items (trending down)
- Debt by severity (zero critical)
- Debt items per 1000 LOC

**Quality Metrics:**
- Test coverage (trending up)
- Cyclomatic complexity (trending down)
- Average file/function size (stable or decreasing)

**Velocity Metrics:**
- Debt items resolved per sprint
- New debt items per sprint (should be low)
- Time to resolve (should decrease)

**Business Metrics:**
- Bug rate (should decrease)
- Feature delivery speed (should increase)
- Developer satisfaction (should increase)

content.content:
1→---
     2→name: test-specialist
     3→description: This skill should be used when writing test cases, fixing bugs, analyzing code for potential issues, or improving test coverage for JavaScript/TypeScript applications. Use this for unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, debugging runtime errors, logic bugs, performance issues, security vulnerabilities, and systematic code analysis.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Test Specialist
     7→
     8→## Neuman Brain Testing Stack
     9→
    10→**Neuman Brain uses Vitest + React Testing Library. Follow these patterns:**
    11→
    12→### Testing Stack
    13→- **Unit Tests:** Vitest (not Jest) - faster, native ESM support
    14→- **Component Tests:** React Testing Library - user-centric queries
    15→- **Test Location:** `src/**/__tests__/` or `*.test.tsx` files
    16→- **Run Tests:** `npm run test` (watch mode: `npm run test:watch`)
    17→- **Coverage:** Target 80%+ for critical features (stores, services, components)
    18→
    19→### Neuman Brain Test Patterns
    20→```typescript
    21→import { render, screen, within } from '@testing-library/react';
    22→import userEvent from '@testing-library/user-event';
    23→import { describe, it, expect, vi } from 'vitest';
    24→
    25→// ✅ GOOD: Test user behavior, not implementation
    26→it('creates task when user clicks Add button', async () => {
    27→  const user = userEvent.setup();
    28→  render(<TaskList />);
    29→
    30→  await user.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /add task/i }));
    31→  await user.type(screen.getByLabelText(/task title/i), 'Buy groceries');
    32→  await user.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i }));
    33→
    34→  expect(screen.getByText('Buy groceries')).toBeInTheDocument();
    35→});
    36→
    37→// ❌ BAD: Don't test implementation details
    38→it('calls addTask function', () => {
    39→  const addTask = vi.fn();
    40→  render(<TaskList addTask={addTask} />);
    41→  // Testing internal function calls, not user outcome
    42→});
    43→```
    44→
    45→### Required Test Coverage (CLAUDE.md Rule #13)
    46→- ✅ **New features:** Write unit tests before marking complete
    47→- ✅ **Bug fixes:** Add regression tests
    48→- ✅ **All changes:** Run `npm run test` and `npm run type-check`
    49→- ✅ **Zustand stores:** Test actions and state updates
    50→- ✅ **IndexedDB services:** Test CRUD operations, quota handling
    51→- ✅ **Components:** Test accessibility (keyboard nav, ARIA), user interactions
    52→
    53→### See Also
    54→- `CLAUDE.md` Rule #13 - Verify Testing
    55→- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Testing strategy
    56→- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-code-reviewer/` - Code quality checks
    57→
    58→---
    59→
    60→## Overview
    61→
    62→Apply systematic testing methodologies and debugging techniques to JavaScript/TypeScript applications. This skill provides comprehensive testing strategies, bug analysis frameworks, and automated tools for identifying coverage gaps and untested code.
    63→
    64→## Core Capabilities
    65→
    66→### 1. Writing Test Cases
    67→
    68→Write comprehensive tests covering unit, integration, and end-to-end scenarios.
    69→
    70→#### Unit Testing Approach
    71→
    72→Structure tests using the AAA pattern (Arrange-Act-Assert):
    73→
    74→```typescript
    75→describe('ExpenseCalculator', () => {
    76→  describe('calculateTotal', () => {
    77→    test('sums expense amounts correctly', () => {
    78→      // Arrange
    79→      const expenses = [
    80→        { amount: 100, category: 'food' },
    81→        { amount: 50, category: 'transport' },
    82→        { amount: 25, category: 'entertainment' }
    83→      ];
    84→
    85→      // Act
    86→      const total = calculateTotal(expenses);
    87→
    88→      // Assert
    89→      expect(total).toBe(175);
    90→    });
    91→
    92→    test('handles empty expense list', () => {
    93→      expect(calculateTotal([])).toBe(0);
    94→    });
    95→
    96→    test('handles negative amounts', () => {
    97→      const expenses = [
    98→        { amount: 100, category: 'food' },
    99→        { amount: -50, category: 'refund' }
   100→      ];
   101→      expect(calculateTotal(expenses)).toBe(50);
   102→    });
   103→  });
   104→});
   105→```
   106→
   107→**Key principles:**
   108→- Test one behavior per test
   109→- Cover happy path, edge cases, and error conditions
   110→- Use descriptive test names that explain the scenario
   111→- Keep tests independent and isolated
   112→
   113→#### Integration Testing Approach
   114→
   115→Test how components work together, including database, API, and service interactions:
   116→
   117→```typescript
   118→describe('ExpenseAPI Integration', () => {
   119→  beforeAll(async () => {
   120→    await database.connect(TEST_DB_URL);
   121→  });
   122→
   123→  afterAll(async () => {
   124→    await database.disconnect();
   125→  });
   126→
   127→  beforeEach(async () => {
   128→    await database.clear();
   129→    await seedTestData();
   130→  });
   131→
   132→  test('POST /expenses creates expense and updates total', async () => {
   133→    const response = await request(app)
   134→      .post('/api/expenses')
   135→      .send({
   136→        amount: 50,
   137→        category: 'food',
   138→        description: 'Lunch'
   139→      })
   140→      .expect(201);
   141→
   142→    expect(response.body).toMatchObject({
   143→      id: expect.any(Number),
   144→      amount: 50,
   145→      category: 'food'
   146→    });
   147→
   148→    // Verify database state
   149→    const total = await getTotalExpenses();
   150→    expect(total).toBe(50);
   151→  });
   152→});
   153→```
   154→
   155→#### End-to-End Testing Approach
   156→
   157→Test complete user workflows using tools like Playwright or Cypress:
   158→
   159→```typescript
   160→test('user can track expense from start to finish', async ({ page }) => {
   161→  // Navigate to app
   162→  await page.goto('/');
   163→
   164→  // Add new expense
   165→  await page.click('[data-testid="add-expense-btn"]');
   166→  await page.fill('[data-testid="amount"]', '50.00');
   167→  await page.selectOption('[data-testid="category"]', 'food');
   168→  await page.fill('[data-testid="description"]', 'Lunch');
   169→  await page.click('[data-testid="submit"]');
   170→
   171→  // Verify expense appears in list
   172→  await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="expense-item"]')).toContainText('Lunch');
   173→  await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="total"]')).toContainText('$50.00');
   174→});
   175→```
   176→
   177→### 2. Systematic Bug Analysis
   178→
   179→Apply structured debugging methodology to identify and fix issues.
   180→
   181→#### Five-Step Analysis Process
   182→
   183→1. **Reproduction**: Reliably reproduce the bug
   184→   - Document exact steps to trigger
   185→   - Identify required environment/state
   186→   - Note expected vs actual behavior
   187→
   188→2. **Isolation**: Narrow down the problem
   189→   - Binary search through code path
   190→   - Create minimal reproduction case
   191→   - Remove unrelated dependencies
   192→
   193→3. **Root Cause Analysis**: Determine underlying cause
   194→   - Trace execution flow
   195→   - Check assumptions and preconditions
   196→   - Review recent changes (git blame)
   197→
   198→4. **Fix Implementation**: Implement solution
   199→   - Write failing test first (TDD)
   200→   - Implement the fix
   201→   - Verify test passes
   202→
   203→5. **Validation**: Ensure completeness
   204→   - Run full test suite
   205→   - Test edge cases
   206→   - Verify no regressions
   207→
   208→#### Common Bug Patterns
   209→
   210→**Race Conditions:**
   211→```typescript
   212→// Test concurrent operations
   213→test('handles concurrent updates correctly', async () => {
   214→  const promises = Array.from({ length: 100 }, () =>
   215→    incrementExpenseCount()
   216→  );
   217→
   218→  await Promise.all(promises);
   219→  expect(getExpenseCount()).toBe(100);
   220→});
   221→```
   222→
   223→**Null/Undefined Errors:**
   224→```typescript
   225→// Test null safety
   226→test.each([null, undefined, '', 0, false])
   227→  ('handles invalid input: %p', (input) => {
   228→    expect(() => processExpense(input)).toThrow('Invalid expense');
   229→  });
   230→```
   231→
   232→**Off-by-One Errors:**
   233→```typescript
   234→// Test boundaries explicitly
   235→describe('pagination', () => {
   236→  test('handles empty list', () => {
   237→    expect(paginate([], 1, 10)).toEqual([]);
   238→  });
   239→
   240→  test('handles single item', () => {
   241→    expect(paginate([item], 1, 10)).toEqual([item]);
   242→  });
   243→
   244→  test('handles last page with partial items', () => {
   245→    const items = Array.from({ length: 25 }, (_, i) => i);
   246→    expect(paginate(items, 3, 10)).toHaveLength(5);
   247→  });
   248→});
   249→```
   250→
   251→### 3. Identifying Potential Issues
   252→
   253→Proactively identify issues before they become bugs.
   254→
   255→#### Security Vulnerabilities
   256→
   257→Test for common security issues:
   258→
   259→```typescript
   260→describe('security', () => {
   261→  test('prevents SQL injection', async () => {
   262→    const malicious = "'; DROP TABLE expenses; --";
   263→    await expect(
   264→      searchExpenses(malicious)
   265→    ).resolves.not.toThrow();
   266→  });
   267→
   268→  test('sanitizes XSS in descriptions', () => {
   269→    const xss = '<script>alert("xss")</script>';
   270→    const expense = createExpense({ description: xss });
   271→    expect(expense.description).not.toContain('<script>');
   272→  });
   273→
   274→  test('requires authentication for expense operations', async () => {
   275→    await request(app)
   276→      .post('/api/expenses')
   277→      .send({ amount: 50 })
   278→      .expect(401);
   279→  });
   280→});
   281→```
   282→
   283→#### Performance Issues
   284→
   285→Test for performance problems:
   286→
   287→```typescript
   288→test('processes large expense list efficiently', () => {
   289→  const largeList = Array.from({ length: 10000 }, (_, i) => ({
   290→    amount: i,
   291→    category: 'test'
   292→  }));
   293→
   294→  const start = performance.now();
   295→  const total = calculateTotal(largeList);
   296→  const duration = performance.now() - start;
   297→
   298→  expect(duration).toBeLessThan(100); // Should complete in <100ms
   299→  expect(total).toBe(49995000);
   300→});
   301→```
   302→
   303→#### Logic Errors
   304→
   305→Use parameterized tests to catch edge cases:
   306→
   307→```typescript
   308→test.each([
   309→  // [input, expected, description]
   310→  [[10, 20, 30], 60, 'normal positive values'],
   311→  [[0, 0, 0], 0, 'all zeros'],
   312→  [[-10, 20, -5], 5, 'mixed positive and negative'],
   313→  [[0.1, 0.2], 0.3, 'decimal precision'],
   314→  [[Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER], Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER, 'large numbers'],
   315→])('calculateTotal(%p) = %p (%s)', (amounts, expected, description) => {
   316→  const expenses = amounts.map(amount => ({ amount, category: 'test' }));
   317→  expect(calculateTotal(expenses)).toBeCloseTo(expected);
   318→});
   319→```
   320→
   321→### 4. Test Coverage Analysis
   322→
   323→Use automated tools to identify gaps in test coverage.
   324→
   325→#### Finding Untested Code
   326→
   327→Run the provided script to identify source files without tests:
   328→
   329→```bash
   330→python3 scripts/find_untested_code.py src
   331→```
   332→
   333→The script will:
   334→- Scan source directory for all code files
   335→- Identify which files lack corresponding test files
   336→- Categorize untested files by type (components, services, utils, etc.)
   337→- Prioritize files that need testing most
   338→
   339→**Interpretation:**
   340→- **API/Services**: High priority - test business logic and data operations
   341→- **Models**: High priority - test data validation and transformations
   342→- **Hooks**: Medium priority - test stateful behavior
   343→- **Components**: Medium priority - test complex UI logic
   344→- **Utils**: Low priority - test as needed for complex functions
   345→
   346→#### Analyzing Coverage Reports
   347→
   348→Run the coverage analysis script after generating coverage:
   349→
   350→```bash
   351→# Generate coverage (using Jest example)
   352→npm test -- --coverage
   353→
   354→# Analyze coverage gaps
   355→python3 scripts/analyze_coverage.py coverage/coverage-final.json
   356→```
   357→
   358→The script identifies:
   359→- Files below coverage threshold (default 80%)
   360→- Statement, branch, and function coverage percentages
   361→- Priority files to improve
   362→
   363→**Coverage targets:**
   364→- Critical paths: 90%+ coverage
   365→- Business logic: 85%+ coverage
   366→- UI components: 75%+ coverage
   367→- Utilities: 70%+ coverage
   368→
   369→### 5. Test Maintenance and Quality
   370→
   371→Ensure tests remain valuable and maintainable.
   372→
   373→#### Test Code Quality Principles
   374→
   375→**DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself):**
   376→```typescript
   377→// Extract common setup
   378→function createTestExpense(overrides = {}) {
   379→  return {
   380→    amount: 50,
   381→    category: 'food',
   382→    description: 'Test expense',
   383→    date: new Date('2024-01-01'),
   384→    ...overrides
   385→  };
   386→}
   387→
   388→test('filters by category', () => {
   389→  const expenses = [
   390→    createTestExpense({ category: 'food' }),
   391→    createTestExpense({ category: 'transport' }),
   392→  ];
   393→  // ...
   394→});
   395→```
   396→
   397→**Clear test data:**
   398→```typescript
   399→// Bad: Magic numbers
   400→expect(calculateDiscount(100, 0.15)).toBe(85);
   401→
   402→// Good: Named constants
   403→const ORIGINAL_PRICE = 100;
   404→const DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.15;
   405→const EXPECTED_PRICE = 85;
   406→expect(calculateDiscount(ORIGINAL_PRICE, DISCOUNT_RATE)).toBe(EXPECTED_PRICE);
   407→```
   408→
   409→**Avoid test interdependence:**
   410→```typescript
   411→// Bad: Tests depend on execution order
   412→let sharedState;
   413→test('test 1', () => {
   414→  sharedState = { value: 1 };
   415→});
   416→test('test 2', () => {
   417→  expect(sharedState.value).toBe(1); // Depends on test 1
   418→});
   419→
   420→// Good: Independent tests
   421→test('test 1', () => {
   422→  const state = { value: 1 };
   423→  expect(state.value).toBe(1);
   424→});
   425→test('test 2', () => {
   426→  const state = { value: 1 };
   427→  expect(state.value).toBe(1);
   428→});
   429→```
   430→
   431→## Workflow Decision Tree
   432→
   433→Follow this decision tree to determine the testing approach:
   434→
   435→1. **Adding new functionality?**
   436→   - Yes → Write tests first (TDD)
   437→     - Write failing test
   438→     - Implement feature
   439→     - Verify test passes
   440→     - Refactor
   441→   - No → Go to step 2
   442→
   443→2. **Fixing a bug?**
   444→   - Yes → Apply bug analysis process
   445→     - Reproduce the bug
   446→     - Write failing test demonstrating bug
   447→     - Fix the implementation
   448→     - Verify test passes
   449→   - No → Go to step 3
   450→
   451→3. **Improving test coverage?**
   452→   - Yes → Use coverage tools
   453→     - Run `find_untested_code.py` to identify gaps
   454→     - Run `analyze_coverage.py` on coverage reports
   455→     - Prioritize critical paths
   456→     - Write tests for untested code
   457→   - No → Go to step 4
   458→
   459→4. **Analyzing code quality?**
   460→   - Yes → Systematic review
   461→     - Check for security vulnerabilities
   462→     - Test edge cases and error handling
   463→     - Verify performance characteristics
   464→     - Review error handling
   465→
   466→## Testing Frameworks and Tools
   467→
   468→### Recommended Stack
   469→
   470→**Unit/Integration Testing:**
   471→- Jest or Vitest for test runner
   472→- Testing Library for React components
   473→- Supertest for API testing
   474→- MSW (Mock Service Worker) for API mocking
   475→
   476→**E2E Testing:**
   477→- Playwright or Cypress
   478→- Page Object Model pattern
   479→
   480→**Coverage:**
   481→- Istanbul (built into Jest/Vitest)
   482→- Coverage reports in JSON format
   483→
   484→### Running Tests
   485→
   486→```bash
   487→# Run all tests
   488→npm test
   489→
   490→# Run with coverage
   491→npm test -- --coverage
   492→
   493→# Run specific test file
   494→npm test -- ExpenseCalculator.test.ts
   495→
   496→# Run in watch mode
   497→npm test -- --watch
   498→
   499→# Run E2E tests
   500→npm run test:e2e
   501→```
   502→
   503→## Reference Documentation
   504→
   505→For detailed patterns and techniques, refer to:
   506→
   507→- `references/testing_patterns.md` - Comprehensive testing patterns, best practices, and code examples
   508→- `references/bug_analysis.md` - In-depth bug analysis framework, common bug patterns, and debugging techniques
   509→
   510→These references contain extensive examples and advanced techniques. Load them when:
   511→- Dealing with complex testing scenarios
   512→- Need specific pattern implementations
   513→- Debugging unusual issues
   514→- Seeking best practices for specific situations
   515→
   516→## Scripts
   517→
   518→### analyze_coverage.py
   519→
   520→Analyze Jest/Istanbul coverage reports to identify gaps:
   521→
   522→```bash
   523→python3 scripts/analyze_coverage.py [coverage-file]
   524→```
   525→
   526→Automatically finds common coverage file locations if not specified.
   527→
   528→**Output:**
   529→- Files below coverage threshold
   530→- Statement, branch, and function coverage percentages
   531→- Priority files to improve
   532→
   533→### find_untested_code.py
   534→
   535→Find source files without corresponding test files:
   536→
   537→```bash
   538→python3 scripts/find_untested_code.py [src-dir] [--pattern test|spec]
   539→```
   540→
   541→**Output:**
   542→- Total source and test file counts
   543→- Test file coverage percentage
   544→- Untested files categorized by type (API, services, components, etc.)
   545→- Recommendations for prioritization
   546→
   547→## Best Practices Summary
   548→
   549→1. **Write tests first** (TDD) when adding new features
   550→2. **Test behavior, not implementation** - tests should survive refactoring
   551→3. **Keep tests independent** - no shared state between tests
   552→4. **Use descriptive names** - test names should explain the scenario
   553→5. **Cover edge cases** - null, empty, boundary values, error conditions
   554→6. **Mock external dependencies** - tests should be fast and reliable
   555→7. **Maintain high coverage** - 80%+ for critical code
   556→8. **Fix failing tests immediately** - never commit broken tests
   557→9. **Refactor tests** - apply same quality standards as production code
   558→10. **Use tools** - automate coverage analysis and gap identification
   559→
   560→---
   561→
   562→## See Also
   563→
   564→- `CLAUDE.md` - Rule #13 (Verify Testing) - **Tests required before ALL commits**
   565→- `.claude/skills/neuman-brain-code-reviewer/` - Pre-commit quality checks
   566→- `docs/technical/development_principles.md` - Testing requirements
   567→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

It provides production-ready components, comprehensive design guidelines, curated color palettes, smooth animations, and flexible layout templates optimized for responsiveness and accessibility.
