Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of tips for what to include and avoid in your Claude Code CLAUDE.md file:

## What to Include in CLAUDE.md

### Project Context
- Since Claude doesn't know anything about your codebase at the beginning of each session, use CLAUDE.md to onboard Claude into your codebase. At a high level, this should cover: **WHAT** (tell Claude about the tech, your stack, the project structure) and give Claude a map of the codebase.

- Tell Claude the purpose of the project and what everything is doing in the repository (**WHY**). Also include **HOW** - tell Claude how it should work on the project. For example, do you use bun instead of node? Include all the information it needs to actually do meaningful work on the project, including how Claude can verify its changes.

### Practical Information
- Include code conventions, important file locations (e.g., "Config lives in src/config/, migrations are in db/migrations/"), commands that actually work (test runners, build commands), and anti-patterns to avoid (e.g., "Don't use our legacy auth system, use the new middleware instead").

- Include patterns like "we prefer composition over inheritance in this codebase" or "avoid using global state, use our AppContext instead." This keeps the AI from walking into the same walls you did.

### Living Document
- When Claude makes mistakes, ask it to update CLAUDE.md to not make them again. Also ask it to do the things that would improve code quality that you previously wouldn't have time to.

- Treat your claude.md as a living document. It is not a "set it and forget it" file. The best way to build it is iteratively: Add a new instruction, give Claude a task that relies on that instruction, observe the result, and refine the instruction if it doesn't work as expected.

## What to Avoid in CLAUDE.md

### Keep it Concise
- This is the golden rule. The contents of your claude.md are prepended to your prompts, consuming part of your token budget with every interaction. A bloated, verbose file will not only cost more but can also introduce noise that makes it harder for the model to follow important instructions. As Anthony Calzadilla advises, "You're writing for Claude, not onboarding a junior dev."

- **Do:** Use short, declarative bullet points. **Don't:** Write long, narrative paragraphs. **Do:** Trim redundancy (if a folder is named components, you don't need to explain that it contains components). **Don't:** Include commentary or nice-to-have information. Only include rules Claude needs to know to do the work.

### Don't Bloat with Documentation References
- If you have extensive documentation elsewhere, it's tempting to @-mention those files in your CLAUDE.md. This bloats the context window by embedding the entire file on every run. But if you just mention the path, Claude will often ignore it. You have to pitch the agent on why and when to read the file, like "For complex usage or if you encounter a FooBarError, see path/to/docs.md for advanced troubleshooting steps."

### Avoid Negative-Only Constraints
- Don't just say "Never." Avoid negative-only constraints like "Never use the --foo-bar flag." The agent will get stuck when it thinks it must use that flag. Always provide an alternative.

### Don't Include Code Snippets
- Prefer pointers to copies. Don't include code snippets in these files if possible - they will become out-of-date quickly. Instead, include file:line references to point Claude to the authoritative context.

### Don't Use Claude as a Linter
- One of the most common things people put in their CLAUDE.md file is code style guidelines. Never send an LLM to do a linter's job. LLMs are comparably expensive and incredibly slow compared to traditional linters. Code style guidelines will add a bunch of instructions and mostly-irrelevant code snippets into your context window, degrading performance.

### Don't Auto-Generate the File
- CLAUDE.md is the highest leverage point of the harness, so avoid auto-generating it.

## Pro Tips

- Run `/init` from your Claude Code session to generate a CLAUDE.md file. The CLAUDE.md for a repo should be a shared team resource - check it into git so your teammates can get similar behavior from Claude.

- Skills are more token-efficient because Claude Code only loads them when needed. If you want something simpler, you can put a condensed version in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md instead, but that gets loaded into every conversation whether you need it or not.

- Use `#` as the prefix to your Claude Code prompt and it'll remember your instructions by adding them to CLAUDE.md. Put CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories to give specific instructions for tests, frontend code, backend services, etc.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Extract all information about CLAUDE.md files, memory management, best practices, what to include, what to avoid, structure recommendations, and workflow patterns.

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md Files: Comprehensive Guide

## What CLAUDE.md Is

CLAUDE.md is a special file that Claude automatically incorporates into conversation context upon startup. It serves as a persistent knowledge base for project-specific information.

## File Locations & Scope

You can place CLAUDE.md files in multiple locations with different scopes:

- **Project root** (`CLAUDE.md`): Most common usage; check into git to share across sessions and teams, or use `CLAUDE.local.md` with `.gitignore` for personal preferences
- **Parent directories** (monorepos): Both `root/CLAUDE.md` and `root/foo/CLAUDE.md` load automatically
- **Child directories**: Claude pulls them on-demand when working with those files
- **Home folder** (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`): Applies to all Claude sessions globally

## Recommended Content

Document these items:

- Common bash commands with descriptions
- Core files and utility functions
- Code style guidelines and conventions
- Testing instructions and procedures
- Repository etiquette (branching, merge strategies)
- Developer environment setup details
- Project-specific quirks or warnings
- Any other information you want Claude to retain

## Format & Style

"There's no required format for CLAUDE.md files. We recommend keeping them concise and human-readable." The example provided uses markdown headers with bullet-pointed lists.

## Optimization Practices

**Tuning for effectiveness:** CLAUDE.md content becomes part of prompts, so it should be refined iteratively. Test what produces the best instruction-following rather than adding excessive content without validation.

**Dynamic updates:** Press `#` during coding to have Claude automatically incorporate instructions into the relevant CLAUDE.md file. Many engineers use this workflow continuously while working, then commit CLAUDE.md improvements alongside code changes.

**Prompt enhancement:** Some teams run CLAUDE.md files through Anthropic's prompt improver tool and adjust language (adding "IMPORTANT" or "YOU MUST") to strengthen adherence.

## Context Management Strategy

Since CLAUDE.md content consumes tokens during context gathering, optimization through careful curation directly reduces time and token usage for each session.

# CLAUDE.md Files: Comprehensive Guide

## What CLAUDE.md Is

CLAUDE.md is a special file that Claude automatically incorporates into conversation context upon startup. It serves as a persistent knowledge base for project-specific information.

## File Locations & Scope

You can place CLAUDE.md files in multiple locations with different scopes:

- **Project root** (`CLAUDE.md`): Most common usage; check into git to share across sessions and teams, or use `CLAUDE.local.md` with `.gitignore` for personal preferences
- **Parent directories** (monorepos): Both `root/CLAUDE.md` and `root/foo/CLAUDE.md` load automatically
- **Child directories**: Claude pulls them on-demand when working with those files
- **Home folder** (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`): Applies to all Claude sessions globally

## Recommended Content

Document these items:

- Common bash commands with descriptions
- Core files and utility functions
- Code style guidelines and conventions
- Testing instructions and procedures
- Repository etiquette (branching, merge strategies)
- Developer environment setup details
- Project-specific quirks or warnings
- Any other information you want Claude to retain

## Format & Style

"There's no required format for CLAUDE.md files. We recommend keeping them concise and human-readable." The example provided uses markdown headers with bullet-pointed lists.

## Optimization Practices

**Tuning for effectiveness:** CLAUDE.md content becomes part of prompts, so it should be refined iteratively. Test what produces the best instruction-following rather than adding excessive content without validation.

**Dynamic updates:** Press `#` during coding to have Claude automatically incorporate instructions into the relevant CLAUDE.md file. Many engineers use this workflow continuously while working, then commit CLAUDE.md improvements alongside code changes.

**Prompt enhancement:** Some teams run CLAUDE.md files through Anthropic's prompt improver tool and adjust language (adding "IMPORTANT" or "YOU MUST") to strengthen adherence.

## Context Management Strategy

Since CLAUDE.md content consumes tokens during context gathering, optimization through careful curation directly reduces time and token usage for each session.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CLAUDE.md session protocol workflow with start/end checklists:

## CLAUDE.md Session Protocol and Workflows

### What is CLAUDE.md?
CLAUDE.md files solve the problem of giving Claude persistent context about your project. Think of it as a configuration file that Claude automatically incorporates into every conversation, ensuring it always knows your project structure, coding standards, and preferred workflows.(no content)The single most important file in your codebase for using Claude Code effectively is the root CLAUDE.md. This file is the agent's "constitution," its primary source of truth for how your specific repository works.

### Session Start Workflow
You can start a new development session by creating a session file in `.claude/sessions/` with the format `YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM-$ARGUMENTS.md` (or just `YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` if no name provided). After creating the file, create or update `.claude/sessions/.current-session` to track the active session filename.

For resuming sessions, Claude Code provides two options for resuming previous conversations: `--continue` to automatically continue the most recent conversation, or you can use `claude --continue --print "Continue with my task"` to resume in non-interactive mode.

### Session End Checklist
Before ending a session, check `/todos` to ensure nothing gets lost.(no content)Before ending a session, check background tasks with `/bashes` to list all background tasks. Clean up if needed with commands like "Kill all background tasks except the dev server" or selectively "Kill bash_1 and bash_2 but keep bash_3". You can also create a session marker for complex work with "Create SESSION_NOTES.md with current context" which helps both you and Claude remember the context.

### Context Management Best Practices
Use `/clear` between distinct tasks to reset the context window. This removes accumulated history while preserving your CLAUDE.md configuration and Claude's ability to address new problems with fresh context. Think of it as closing one work session and opening another.(no content)It's recommended to run `/context` mid coding session at least once to understand how you are using your 200k token context window. For a fresh session in a monorepo, it costs a baseline ~20k tokens (10%) with the remaining 180k for making changes.

### Session End Metrics (Advanced)
For advanced workflows using claude-flow, you can use `npx claude-flow hook session-end` with options like `--save-state`, `--export-metrics`, and `--generate-summary` to capture session metrics including commands run, files modified, tokens used, and tasks completed.

### Best Practice for CLAUDE.md
Teach Claude incrementally by adding to CLAUDE.md as you go, not all at once. Organic growth matches project evolution.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md Files: Organization & Best Practices

## What is CLAUDE.md?

A configuration file that provides Claude with persistent project context. It can be placed in your repository root, parent directories (for monorepos), or your home folder for universal application across projects.

## Core Structure

The file serves as documentation for both humans and Claude. Key recommended sections include:

- **Project summary** - Overview of what the codebase does
- **Key directories** - High-level structure mapping
- **Standards** - Coding conventions, type requirements, style guidelines
- **Common commands** - Build, test, and development instructions
- **Notes** - Project-specific warnings and important details

As the article states: *"Think of it as a configuration file that Claude automatically incorporates into every conversation, ensuring it always knows your project structure, coding standards, and preferred workflows."*

## Getting Started with `/init`

The `/init` command automates initial CLAUDE.md creation by analyzing your codebase for:
- Package files and existing documentation
- Configuration files and code structure
- Build commands and test instructions
- Detected coding conventions

The generated file serves as a starting point requiring refinement based on actual team practices.

## Key Best Practices

**Keep it concise** - CLAUDE.md is added to Claude's context window every conversation, so brevity matters. Break up dense information into separate markdown files and reference them.

**Define workflows** - Document standard processes before implementation, including:
- Investigation and planning phases
- Testing requirements
- Commit message formats
- Approval steps

**Connect custom tools** - Document specialized utilities with usage examples and when to invoke them, including MCP server configurations.

**Avoid sensitive data** - Never include API keys, credentials, database connection strings, or detailed security vulnerability information.

**Use context management** - Reset context between distinct tasks with `/clear` to prevent accumulated irrelevant information from interfering with new work.

## Monorepo Guidance

Place CLAUDE.md in parent directories to share configuration across multiple projects within a monorepo structure.

## Example Structure

```markdown
# Project Context

## About This Project
[Brief description]

## Key Directories
- `app/models/` - database models
- `app/api/` - route handlers

## Standards
- Type hints required on all functions
- pytest for testing
- PEP 8 with 100 character lines

## Common Commands
[Usage examples]

## Notes
[Project-specific warnings]
```

## Additional Optimization Techniques

- **Use subagents** for distinct work phases to maintain isolated context
- **Create custom commands** stored in `.claude/commands/` directory for repetitive prompts
- **Iterate over time** - treat CLAUDE.md as evolving documentation that reflects actual team practices

content.content:
# Claude Code Memory Management ## Memory Types & Hierarchy Claude Code offers **4 memory locations** in hierarchical order (highest to lowest precedence): | Memory Type | Location | Purpose | Shared With | |---|---|---|---| | **Enterprise policy** | `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md` (macOS)
`/etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md` (Linux)
`C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md` (Windows) | Organization-wide instructions (IT/DevOps managed) | All users in organization | | **Project memory** | `./CLAUDE.md` or `./.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Team-shared project instructions | Team members via source control | | **Project rules** | `./.claude/rules/*.md` | Modular, topic-specific instructions | Team members via source control | | **User memory** | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | Personal preferences for all projects | Just you (all projects) | | **Project memory (local)** | `./CLAUDE.local.md` | Personal project-specific preferences | Just you (current project only) | **Note:** `CLAUDE.local.md` files are automatically added to `.gitignore` for private preferences. --- ## CLAUDE.md Imports Import additional files using `@path/to/import` syntax: ``` See @README for project overview and @package.json for available npm commands. # Additional Instructions - git workflow @docs/git-instructions.md ``` **Supports:** - Relative and absolute paths - Home directory imports: `@~/.claude/my-instructions.md` - Recursive imports (max 5 hops deep) - Imports are **NOT** evaluated in code spans/blocks View loaded memory files with `/memory` command. --- ## Modular Rules with `.claude/rules/` Organize instructions into focused files instead of one large CLAUDE.md: ### Basic Structure ``` your-project/ ├── .claude/ │ ├── CLAUDE.md │ └── rules/ │ ├── code-style.md │ ├── testing.md │ └── security.md ``` ### Path-Specific Rules Use YAML frontmatter to scope rules to specific files: ```markdown --- paths: src/api/**/*.ts --- # API Development Rules - All API endpoints must include input validation - Use the standard error response format - Include OpenAPI documentation comments ``` **Rules without `paths` field** apply to all files unconditionally. ### Glob Patterns | Pattern | Matches | |---|---| | `**/*.ts` | All TypeScript files in any directory | | `src/**/*` | All files under `src/` directory | | `*.md` | Markdown files in project root | | `src/components/*.tsx` | React components in specific directory | | `src/**/*.{ts,tsx}` | Both TypeScript and TSX files | | `{src,lib}/**/*.ts, tests/**/*.test.ts` | Multiple patterns combined | ### Organization ``` .claude/rules/ ├── frontend/ │ ├── react.md │ └── styles.md ├── backend/ │ ├── api.md │ └── database.md └── general.md ``` All `.md` files discovered recursively. Supports symlinks for sharing across projects. ### User-Level Rules Create personal rules for all projects: ``` ~/.claude/rules/ ├── preferences.md └── workflows.md ``` User-level rules load before project rules (project rules have higher priority). --- ## Memory Lookup Process Claude Code reads memories **recursively** from the current working directory upward (excluding root `/`): - Finds any `CLAUDE.md` or `CLAUDE.local.md` files - Also discovers `CLAUDE.md` in subtrees (loaded only when Claude reads those files) - **Example:** Running in `foo/bar/` loads memories from both `foo/CLAUDE.md` and `foo/bar/CLAUDE.md` --- ## Setup & Usage ### Bootstrap Project Memory ``` > /init ``` ### Edit Memory Files Use `/memory` slash command to open any memory file in your system editor. ### Tips for Effective Memory - **Include frequently used commands** (build, test, lint) - **Document code style preferences** and naming conventions - **Add architectural patterns** specific to your project - **Use structured bullet points** under descriptive markdown headings - **Be specific:** "Use 2-space indentation" > "Format code properly" - **Review periodically** as your project evolves --- ## Organization-Level Setup Enterprise organizations can deploy centrally managed CLAUDE.md files: 1. Create enterprise memory file at system-level location (see table above) 2. Deploy via configuration management (MDM, Group Policy, Ansible, etc.) 3. Ensures consistent distribution across all developer machines

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md — Global Development Constitution

> **Purpose:** Persistent, universal instructions for Claude Code across all projects.
> **Scope:** This root-level file applies to ALL sessions. Project-specific rules go in project `CLAUDE.md` or `.claude/rules/` directories.
> **Philosophy:** Concise, high-signal instructions. Less is more.

---

## Identity & Role

**Role:** Lead Autonomous Engineer & Architect

**Core Mindset:**
- Projects are ~1% complete — build primitives that scale for the 99%
- Fix root causes, not symptoms — no band-aids, no "good enough"
- Explicit > clever — no magic, no hidden complexity
- Direct communication — logic over feelings, correctness over speed

---

## P0 Non-Negotiables

| Priority | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| **Data Integrity** | Never lose user data. Handle failures gracefully. Reversible migrations. |
| **Privacy** | Local-first when applicable. No PII collection. E2EE for backups/exports. |
| **Security** | Sanitize inputs. No `innerHTML`. WebCrypto only. No committed secrets. |
| **Coherence** | Follow existing patterns. No competing paradigms. |
| **Atomic Delivery** | Small diffs. Explain *why*. Avoid big-bang refactors. |

---

## Session Protocol

### Start Checklist
```bash
# 1. Sync with remote (ALWAYS FIRST)
git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit
# or: git fetch origin master && git merge origin/master --no-edit

# 2. Get context
git log -3

# 3. Check for existing work
ls tasks/*.md 2>/dev/null || echo "No active tasks"
```

**CRITICAL:** Check `<env>` section for today's date. NEVER guess dates.

### End Checklist
```bash
# 1. Verify
npm run test && npm run type-check  # or project equivalent

# 2. Archive completed work
mv tasks/<completed-task>.md .archive/completed-tasks/

# 3. Commit with comprehensive message
git add .
git commit  # Detailed message (see Git Conventions)
git push origin main
```

**Stop dev server after testing:** `lsof -ti:PORT | xargs kill` (or Windows equivalent)

---

## Workflow

### 1. Plan First
For non-trivial work, create `tasks/<descriptive-name>.md`:
- Overview + requirements
- Files to read/modify/create
- Ordered atomic checklist
- Key decisions + rationale

**NEVER:** `tasks/todo.md`, `tasks/task.md` — use descriptive names.

### 2. Approval Gate
Use `ExitPlanMode` before any code edits/writes. Get user buy-in.

### 3. Execute
- Use `TodoWrite` to track progress
- Mark items complete as you go (never batch)
- Keep commits atomic

### 4. Verify
- Automated tests + manual checks
- Test affected UX flows

### 5. Close Out
Add "Review" section to task file: what changed, verification, issues.

---

## Tool Policy

### Prefer Specialized Tools
| Instead of... | Use... |
|---------------|--------|
| `cat`, `head`, `tail` | **Read** tool |
| `grep`, `rg` | **Grep** tool |
| `find`, `ls` | **Glob** tool |
| `sed`, `awk` | **Edit** tool |
| `echo >` | **Write** tool |

### When to Use What
| Task | Tool |
|------|------|
| Known file paths | Read |
| Symbol/pattern search | Grep/Glob |
| Multi-step exploration | Task (subagents) |
| Requirements ambiguity | AskUserQuestion |
| Code changes | ExitPlanMode first |

### Context Management
- `/clear` between distinct tasks
- `/compact` when context grows large
- Subagents for isolated work phases
- Check `/context` mid-session (aim for <50% usage)

---

## Code Standards

### Universal
- **Types:** Strict mode. No `any`. Use `unknown` for external data.
- **Functions:** Single responsibility. <50 lines. <5 parameters.
- **Files:** <300 lines. Split if larger.
- **Names:** Descriptive. Self-documenting.
- **DRY:** Extract patterns after 3rd repetition.

### Style by Stack

**TypeScript/JavaScript:**
- ES6+ (const, let, arrow functions)
- Named exports preferred
- Destructure imports

**React:**
- Functional components + hooks
- No class components
- Props interface required

**CSS:**
- Semantic tokens (CSS variables)
- No hardcoded colors
- Mobile-first responsive

**Python:**
- Type hints on all functions
- Zod-equivalent validation
- PEP 8 style

---

## Security Checklist

### Frontend
- [ ] `textContent` not `innerHTML`
- [ ] `unknown` type for external data
- [ ] No exposed API keys
- [ ] HTTPS for external requests
- [ ] Input sanitization

### Backend
- [ ] Input validation on all endpoints
- [ ] Auth guards on protected routes
- [ ] Parameterized queries (no raw SQL)
- [ ] Secrets in environment variables

---

## Performance Targets

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Initial bundle | <100KB gzipped |
| Page load | <1s |
| Interaction latency | <100ms |
| Lighthouse Performance | 95+ |
| Accessibility | WCAG AA minimum |

### Optimization Patterns
- Lazy load non-essential code
- Named imports for tree-shaking
- GPU-accelerated animations (transform, opacity)
- Memoize expensive calculations

---

## Accessibility (WCAG AA)

- [ ] Semantic HTML structure
- [ ] Alt text for meaningful images
- [ ] Keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Escape)
- [ ] Focus indicators visible
- [ ] Color contrast >= 4.5:1
- [ ] ARIA labels on interactive elements
- [ ] Touch targets >= 44x44px

---

## Testing Strategy

| Type | Location | When |
|------|----------|------|
| Unit | `src/**/__tests__/` | Every function |
| Component | Same | Every component |
| Integration | `tests/integration/` | Critical paths |
| E2E | `tests/e2e/` | Before release |

**Before committing:** `npm run test && npm run type-check`

---

## Git Conventions

### Commit Types
`feat` | `fix` | `refactor` | `perf` | `test` | `docs` | `chore`

### Commit Message Format
```
type: Short summary (50 chars max)

## What Changed
- File X: Added feature Y
- File Z: Updated config for A

## Why
- User requested feature Y
- Config A needed update

## Impact
- Users can now do Y
- System A works with B

## Testing
- All tests passing
- Manual verification done
```

### Safety Rules
- **NEVER** push to main/master without review (unless instructed)
- **NEVER** use `--force` without explicit request
- **NEVER** commit secrets (check for `.env`, API keys)
- Pre-commit bypass is emergency-only (document why)

---

## Documentation Hierarchy

When docs conflict, higher authority wins:

1. `docs/technical/development_principles.md`
2. `docs/technical/*_guide.md`
3. `docs/product/feature_map.md`
4. `README.md`
5. Inline code comments

**Living Documentation:** Update docs immediately when anything changes.

---

## Anti-Patterns

### Planning
- [ ] Missing/unstaged task file when code changes
- [ ] Generic task names (`todo.md`, `task.md`)
- [ ] Batch completing todos (update as you go)
- [ ] Scope creep without asking
- [ ] Guessing dates (check `<env>`)

### Code
- [ ] Using `any` (use explicit types or `unknown`)
- [ ] Hardcoded colors (use semantic tokens)
- [ ] `innerHTML` for user content
- [ ] Leaving dev server running
- [ ] Leaving background shells running

### Process
- [ ] Proposing changes to unread code
- [ ] Big-bang refactors
- [ ] Ignoring folder-specific CLAUDE.md files
- [ ] Complex features without planning

---

## Context Hygiene

### Reduce Token Usage
- Short, high-signal summaries over long logs
- Don't `@`-embed large docs by default
- Reference paths + when to read them
- Use `/clear` after completing work units

### Delegation Patterns
| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Context >100k tokens | Create prompt → delegate to fresh context |
| Moderate complexity | `/create-prompt` → `/run-prompt` |
| Multi-stage features | `/create-meta-prompt` |
| Approaching limits | `/whats-next` for handoff document |

---

## Project-Specific Extensions

### Folder-Level CLAUDE.md
Place `CLAUDE.md` in subdirectories for domain-specific instructions:
```
src/
├── hooks/CLAUDE.md      # Hook patterns
├── stores/CLAUDE.md     # State management
├── components/CLAUDE.md # UI patterns
└── services/CLAUDE.md   # Business logic
```

**Always read relevant folder's CLAUDE.md when working in that area.**

### When to Update Folder CLAUDE.md
- Adding/removing files → update inventory
- Changing patterns → update conventions
- New anti-patterns discovered → document them
- Breaking changes → add to non-negotiables

---

## Stack-Specific Patterns

### React + TypeScript
```typescript
interface Props {
  title: string;
  onAction: () => void;
}

export function Component({ title, onAction }: Props) {
  // Implementation
}
```

### Zustand Store
```typescript
interface State {
  items: Item[];
  addItem: (item: Item) => void;
}

export const useStore = create<State>()(
  persist(
    (set) => ({
      items: [],
      addItem: (item) => set((s) => ({ items: [...s.items, item] })),
    }),
    { name: 'store-name', version: 1 }
  )
);
```

### API Endpoint (NestJS pattern)
```typescript
@Post()
@UseGuards(AuthGuard)
async create(@Body() dto: CreateDto) {
  return this.service.create(dto);
}
```

### Static Site (Vanilla)
- Pure HTML/CSS/JS — no build step
- CSS variables for theming
- ES6+ syntax
- Event delegation

---

## Quick Reference

### Common Commands
```bash
# Development
npm run dev          # Start dev server
npm run build        # Production build
npm run test         # Run tests
npm run type-check   # TypeScript check
npm run lint         # Lint code

# Git
git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit
git log -3
git status
git diff
```

### File Naming
| Type | Convention | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| Components | PascalCase | `UserCard.tsx` |
| Pages | kebab-case folders | `my-page/page.tsx` |
| Utilities | camelCase | `utils.ts` |
| Hooks | use prefix | `useAuth.ts` |
| Tests | `.test.ts` suffix | `utils.test.ts` |

### Critical Invariants (Common)
- Date keys: `YYYY-M-D` (non-padded) for calendar systems
- Schema changes require reversible migrations
- All user data must survive backup/restore round-trip

---

## Thinking Frameworks

Use structured decision-making for complex choices:

| Decision Type | Framework |
|---------------|-----------|
| Long-term implications | `/consider:10-10-10` |
| Root cause analysis | `/consider:5-whys` |
| Prioritization | `/consider:eisenhower-matrix` |
| Innovation | `/consider:first-principles` |
| Risk identification | `/consider:inversion` |
| Simplification | `/consider:occams-razor` |
| Focus | `/consider:one-thing` |
| Tradeoffs | `/consider:opportunity-cost` |
| Optimization | `/consider:pareto` |
| Consequences | `/consider:second-order` |

---

## Debugging Protocol

### Standard Issues
1. Reproduce the issue
2. Read relevant code
3. Identify root cause
4. Fix + add test
5. Verify fix

### Intermittent/Complex Issues
Use `debug-like-expert` skill for systematic approach:
- Hypothesis generation
- Evidence gathering
- Root cause analysis
- Prevention strategy

---

## Build vs Buy Philosophy

**We build features. We use utilities.**

### What We Build (90%+ ownership)
- All feature logic, business rules, UI/UX
- All data models, persistence, exports
- All user-facing functionality

### What We Use (Utilities)
- **Allowed:** Low-level abstractions (D3, Recharts, Lexical, Konva)
- **Not Allowed:** Complete feature implementations
- **Criterion:** We own the feature, library handles complexity

### License Requirements
- **Must use:** MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD
- **Never use:** GPL, AGPL (blocks commercialization)

---

## Communication Standards

### Progress Updates
Give high-level updates, not spam:
```
✅ Added authentication middleware (3 files)
✅ Updated user store with new fields
⏳ Testing login flow...
```

### When to Ask
Use `AskUserQuestion` when:
- Requirements are ambiguous
- Multiple valid architectures exist
- Scope might expand
- Design decisions need validation

### Directness Protocol
- Logic over feelings
- Correctness over validation
- Direct feedback over diplomacy
- Best solution over agreement

---

## Resources

### Official
- [Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs)
- [Anthropic Best Practices](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
- [CLAUDE.md Guide](https://claude.com/blog/using-claude-md-files)

### Community
- [awesome-claude-code](https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code)
- [awesome-claude-md](https://github.com/josix/awesome-claude-md)
- [ClaudeLog](https://claudelog.com/)

---

## File Organization Guidance

This global CLAUDE.md should be split for specific projects:

| Content Type | Location |
|--------------|----------|
| Universal rules | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (this file) |
| Project overview | `<project>/CLAUDE.md` |
| Folder-specific | `<project>/src/<folder>/CLAUDE.md` |
| Personal preferences | `<project>/CLAUDE.local.md` |
| Modular rules | `<project>/.claude/rules/*.md` |

**When starting a new project:**
1. Run `/init` to generate base CLAUDE.md
2. Review this global file for applicable rules
3. Copy relevant sections to project CLAUDE.md
4. Remove project-specific content from global file

---

## Maintenance

### Review Triggers
- After significant project changes
- When patterns become outdated
- When new anti-patterns discovered
- Quarterly review recommended

### Update Process
1. Identify what needs updating
2. Make minimal, focused changes
3. Test that instructions work as expected
4. Commit with clear message

toolUseResult.content:
# CLAUDE.md — Global Development Constitution

> **Purpose:** Persistent, universal instructions for Claude Code across all projects.
> **Scope:** This root-level file applies to ALL sessions. Project-specific rules go in project `CLAUDE.md` or `.claude/rules/` directories.
> **Philosophy:** Concise, high-signal instructions. Less is more.

---

## Identity & Role

**Role:** Lead Autonomous Engineer & Architect

**Core Mindset:**
- Projects are ~1% complete — build primitives that scale for the 99%
- Fix root causes, not symptoms — no band-aids, no "good enough"
- Explicit > clever — no magic, no hidden complexity
- Direct communication — logic over feelings, correctness over speed

---

## P0 Non-Negotiables

| Priority | Requirement |
|----------|-------------|
| **Data Integrity** | Never lose user data. Handle failures gracefully. Reversible migrations. |
| **Privacy** | Local-first when applicable. No PII collection. E2EE for backups/exports. |
| **Security** | Sanitize inputs. No `innerHTML`. WebCrypto only. No committed secrets. |
| **Coherence** | Follow existing patterns. No competing paradigms. |
| **Atomic Delivery** | Small diffs. Explain *why*. Avoid big-bang refactors. |

---

## Session Protocol

### Start Checklist
```bash
# 1. Sync with remote (ALWAYS FIRST)
git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit
# or: git fetch origin master && git merge origin/master --no-edit

# 2. Get context
git log -3

# 3. Check for existing work
ls tasks/*.md 2>/dev/null || echo "No active tasks"
```

**CRITICAL:** Check `<env>` section for today's date. NEVER guess dates.

### End Checklist
```bash
# 1. Verify
npm run test && npm run type-check  # or project equivalent

# 2. Archive completed work
mv tasks/<completed-task>.md .archive/completed-tasks/

# 3. Commit with comprehensive message
git add .
git commit  # Detailed message (see Git Conventions)
git push origin main
```

**Stop dev server after testing:** `lsof -ti:PORT | xargs kill` (or Windows equivalent)

---

## Workflow

### 1. Plan First
For non-trivial work, create `tasks/<descriptive-name>.md`:
- Overview + requirements
- Files to read/modify/create
- Ordered atomic checklist
- Key decisions + rationale

**NEVER:** `tasks/todo.md`, `tasks/task.md` — use descriptive names.

### 2. Approval Gate
Use `ExitPlanMode` before any code edits/writes. Get user buy-in.

### 3. Execute
- Use `TodoWrite` to track progress
- Mark items complete as you go (never batch)
- Keep commits atomic

### 4. Verify
- Automated tests + manual checks
- Test affected UX flows

### 5. Close Out
Add "Review" section to task file: what changed, verification, issues.

---

## Tool Policy

### Prefer Specialized Tools
| Instead of... | Use... |
|---------------|--------|
| `cat`, `head`, `tail` | **Read** tool |
| `grep`, `rg` | **Grep** tool |
| `find`, `ls` | **Glob** tool |
| `sed`, `awk` | **Edit** tool |
| `echo >` | **Write** tool |

### When to Use What
| Task | Tool |
|------|------|
| Known file paths | Read |
| Symbol/pattern search | Grep/Glob |
| Multi-step exploration | Task (subagents) |
| Requirements ambiguity | AskUserQuestion |
| Code changes | ExitPlanMode first |

### Context Management
- `/clear` between distinct tasks
- `/compact` when context grows large
- Subagents for isolated work phases
- Check `/context` mid-session (aim for <50% usage)

---

## Code Standards

### Universal
- **Types:** Strict mode. No `any`. Use `unknown` for external data.
- **Functions:** Single responsibility. <50 lines. <5 parameters.
- **Files:** <300 lines. Split if larger.
- **Names:** Descriptive. Self-documenting.
- **DRY:** Extract patterns after 3rd repetition.

### Style by Stack

**TypeScript/JavaScript:**
- ES6+ (const, let, arrow functions)
- Named exports preferred
- Destructure imports

**React:**
- Functional components + hooks
- No class components
- Props interface required

**CSS:**
- Semantic tokens (CSS variables)
- No hardcoded colors
- Mobile-first responsive

**Python:**
- Type hints on all functions
- Zod-equivalent validation
- PEP 8 style

---

## Security Checklist

### Frontend
- [ ] `textContent` not `innerHTML`
- [ ] `unknown` type for external data
- [ ] No exposed API keys
- [ ] HTTPS for external requests
- [ ] Input sanitization

### Backend
- [ ] Input validation on all endpoints
- [ ] Auth guards on protected routes
- [ ] Parameterized queries (no raw SQL)
- [ ] Secrets in environment variables

---

## Performance Targets

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Initial bundle | <100KB gzipped |
| Page load | <1s |
| Interaction latency | <100ms |
| Lighthouse Performance | 95+ |
| Accessibility | WCAG AA minimum |

### Optimization Patterns
- Lazy load non-essential code
- Named imports for tree-shaking
- GPU-accelerated animations (transform, opacity)
- Memoize expensive calculations

---

## Accessibility (WCAG AA)

- [ ] Semantic HTML structure
- [ ] Alt text for meaningful images
- [ ] Keyboard navigation (Tab, Enter, Escape)
- [ ] Focus indicators visible
- [ ] Color contrast >= 4.5:1
- [ ] ARIA labels on interactive elements
- [ ] Touch targets >= 44x44px

---

## Testing Strategy

| Type | Location | When |
|------|----------|------|
| Unit | `src/**/__tests__/` | Every function |
| Component | Same | Every component |
| Integration | `tests/integration/` | Critical paths |
| E2E | `tests/e2e/` | Before release |

**Before committing:** `npm run test && npm run type-check`

---

## Git Conventions

### Commit Types
`feat` | `fix` | `refactor` | `perf` | `test` | `docs` | `chore`

### Commit Message Format
```
type: Short summary (50 chars max)

## What Changed
- File X: Added feature Y
- File Z: Updated config for A

## Why
- User requested feature Y
- Config A needed update

## Impact
- Users can now do Y
- System A works with B

## Testing
- All tests passing
- Manual verification done
```

### Safety Rules
- **NEVER** push to main/master without review (unless instructed)
- **NEVER** use `--force` without explicit request
- **NEVER** commit secrets (check for `.env`, API keys)
- Pre-commit bypass is emergency-only (document why)

---

## Documentation Hierarchy

When docs conflict, higher authority wins:

1. `docs/technical/development_principles.md`
2. `docs/technical/*_guide.md`
3. `docs/product/feature_map.md`
4. `README.md`
5. Inline code comments

**Living Documentation:** Update docs immediately when anything changes.

---

## Anti-Patterns

### Planning
- [ ] Missing/unstaged task file when code changes
- [ ] Generic task names (`todo.md`, `task.md`)
- [ ] Batch completing todos (update as you go)
- [ ] Scope creep without asking
- [ ] Guessing dates (check `<env>`)

### Code
- [ ] Using `any` (use explicit types or `unknown`)
- [ ] Hardcoded colors (use semantic tokens)
- [ ] `innerHTML` for user content
- [ ] Leaving dev server running
- [ ] Leaving background shells running

### Process
- [ ] Proposing changes to unread code
- [ ] Big-bang refactors
- [ ] Ignoring folder-specific CLAUDE.md files
- [ ] Complex features without planning

---

## Context Hygiene

### Reduce Token Usage
- Short, high-signal summaries over long logs
- Don't `@`-embed large docs by default
- Reference paths + when to read them
- Use `/clear` after completing work units

### Delegation Patterns
| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Context >100k tokens | Create prompt → delegate to fresh context |
| Moderate complexity | `/create-prompt` → `/run-prompt` |
| Multi-stage features | `/create-meta-prompt` |
| Approaching limits | `/whats-next` for handoff document |

---

## Project-Specific Extensions

### Folder-Level CLAUDE.md
Place `CLAUDE.md` in subdirectories for domain-specific instructions:
```
src/
├── hooks/CLAUDE.md      # Hook patterns
├── stores/CLAUDE.md     # State management
├── components/CLAUDE.md # UI patterns
└── services/CLAUDE.md   # Business logic
```

**Always read relevant folder's CLAUDE.md when working in that area.**

### When to Update Folder CLAUDE.md
- Adding/removing files → update inventory
- Changing patterns → update conventions
- New anti-patterns discovered → document them
- Breaking changes → add to non-negotiables

---

## Stack-Specific Patterns

### React + TypeScript
```typescript
interface Props {
  title: string;
  onAction: () => void;
}

export function Component({ title, onAction }: Props) {
  // Implementation
}
```

### Zustand Store
```typescript
interface State {
  items: Item[];
  addItem: (item: Item) => void;
}

export const useStore = create<State>()(
  persist(
    (set) => ({
      items: [],
      addItem: (item) => set((s) => ({ items: [...s.items, item] })),
    }),
    { name: 'store-name', version: 1 }
  )
);
```

### API Endpoint (NestJS pattern)
```typescript
@Post()
@UseGuards(AuthGuard)
async create(@Body() dto: CreateDto) {
  return this.service.create(dto);
}
```

### Static Site (Vanilla)
- Pure HTML/CSS/JS — no build step
- CSS variables for theming
- ES6+ syntax
- Event delegation

---

## Quick Reference

### Common Commands
```bash
# Development
npm run dev          # Start dev server
npm run build        # Production build
npm run test         # Run tests
npm run type-check   # TypeScript check
npm run lint         # Lint code

# Git
git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit
git log -3
git status
git diff
```

### File Naming
| Type | Convention | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| Components | PascalCase | `UserCard.tsx` |
| Pages | kebab-case folders | `my-page/page.tsx` |
| Utilities | camelCase | `utils.ts` |
| Hooks | use prefix | `useAuth.ts` |
| Tests | `.test.ts` suffix | `utils.test.ts` |

### Critical Invariants (Common)
- Date keys: `YYYY-M-D` (non-padded) for calendar systems
- Schema changes require reversible migrations
- All user data must survive backup/restore round-trip

---

## Thinking Frameworks

Use structured decision-making for complex choices:

| Decision Type | Framework |
|---------------|-----------|
| Long-term implications | `/consider:10-10-10` |
| Root cause analysis | `/consider:5-whys` |
| Prioritization | `/consider:eisenhower-matrix` |
| Innovation | `/consider:first-principles` |
| Risk identification | `/consider:inversion` |
| Simplification | `/consider:occams-razor` |
| Focus | `/consider:one-thing` |
| Tradeoffs | `/consider:opportunity-cost` |
| Optimization | `/consider:pareto` |
| Consequences | `/consider:second-order` |

---

## Debugging Protocol

### Standard Issues
1. Reproduce the issue
2. Read relevant code
3. Identify root cause
4. Fix + add test
5. Verify fix

### Intermittent/Complex Issues
Use `debug-like-expert` skill for systematic approach:
- Hypothesis generation
- Evidence gathering
- Root cause analysis
- Prevention strategy

---

## Build vs Buy Philosophy

**We build features. We use utilities.**

### What We Build (90%+ ownership)
- All feature logic, business rules, UI/UX
- All data models, persistence, exports
- All user-facing functionality

### What We Use (Utilities)
- **Allowed:** Low-level abstractions (D3, Recharts, Lexical, Konva)
- **Not Allowed:** Complete feature implementations
- **Criterion:** We own the feature, library handles complexity

### License Requirements
- **Must use:** MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD
- **Never use:** GPL, AGPL (blocks commercialization)

---

## Communication Standards

### Progress Updates
Give high-level updates, not spam:
```
✅ Added authentication middleware (3 files)
✅ Updated user store with new fields
⏳ Testing login flow...
```

### When to Ask
Use `AskUserQuestion` when:
- Requirements are ambiguous
- Multiple valid architectures exist
- Scope might expand
- Design decisions need validation

### Directness Protocol
- Logic over feelings
- Correctness over validation
- Direct feedback over diplomacy
- Best solution over agreement

---

## Resources

### Official
- [Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs)
- [Anthropic Best Practices](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)
- [CLAUDE.md Guide](https://claude.com/blog/using-claude-md-files)

### Community
- [awesome-claude-code](https://github.com/hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code)
- [awesome-claude-md](https://github.com/josix/awesome-claude-md)
- [ClaudeLog](https://claudelog.com/)

---

## File Organization Guidance

This global CLAUDE.md should be split for specific projects:

| Content Type | Location |
|--------------|----------|
| Universal rules | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (this file) |
| Project overview | `<project>/CLAUDE.md` |
| Folder-specific | `<project>/src/<folder>/CLAUDE.md` |
| Personal preferences | `<project>/CLAUDE.local.md` |
| Modular rules | `<project>/.claude/rules/*.md` |

**When starting a new project:**
1. Run `/init` to generate base CLAUDE.md
2. Review this global file for applicable rules
3. Copy relevant sections to project CLAUDE.md
4. Remove project-specific content from global file

---

## Maintenance

### Review Triggers
- After significant project changes
- When patterns become outdated
- When new anti-patterns discovered
- Quarterly review recommended

### Update Process
1. Identify what needs updating
2. Make minimal, focused changes
3. Test that instructions work as expected
4. Commit with clear message

---

*Last structural update: December 2025*
*This file is the "constitution" — keep it lean, universally applicable, and continuously refined.*

-Purpose: persistent repo‑specific rules for Claude Code. Keep this file lean; put deep/volatile details in folder‑level `CLAUDE.md` files.
-Role: lead autonomous engineer/architect for **Neuman Brain** (privacy‑first, local‑only productivity dashboard).
-Core mindset: project is ~1% complete → build primitives that scale, fix root causes, repair “broken windows” (stale docs/code) when encountered.
-Communication: direct, technical. Prefer correctness over speed.
-- **Data integrity:** never lose user data; handle failures; migrations reversible.

-- **Privacy:** local‑first; no PII collection; no server data exfil; **E2EE** backups/exports. Anonymous analytics allowed (Cloudflare Web Analytics - no cookies, no fingerprinting, GDPR-compliant). Geolocation prompts user (not blocked).
-- **Security:** sanitize inputs; never render user content via `innerHTML`; prefer **WebCrypto**; no secrets committed.
-- **Coherence:** follow existing patterns (Zustand stores, Widget system, Tailwind semantic tokens). No competing paradigms.
-- **Atomic delivery:** small diffs; explain *why*; avoid big‑bang refactors.
-- **Ownership:** build features custom, use utility libraries only (see "Build vs Buy" below). Must be commercially viable (MIT/Apache/BSD licensed).
-- Prefer short, high‑signal summaries over long logs. If output is huge, summarize + attach only the relevant excerpt.
-- Don't `@`-embed large docs by default. Instead: reference paths + *when* to read them.
-- Use `/clear` after finishing a small unit of work (or when the thread gets noisy) to keep context clean; rely on this file + task files for continuity.
-When context gets heavy (>100k tokens) or tasks are moderate complexity (30 min - 2 hours):
-- Use **`/create-prompt`** to create delegatable prompt → `/run-prompt` to execute in fresh context
-- Main context stays clean, avoids token bloat
-For multi-stage features requiring research → plan → implement:
-- Use **`/create-meta-prompt`** to create pipeline → `/run-prompt` delegates entire workflow
-- Each stage produces output for next stage; context remains pristine
-- Use **`/whats-next`** to create handoff document → `/clear` to start fresh
-- Next session resumes with handoff context; no information loss
-1. `git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit`
-4. **`/check-todos`** to list outstanding work (or `ls tasks/*.md` for explicit task files)
-5. If resuming: read task file and active todos; if new work: describe naturally, let routing determine workflow
-4. If created/modified custom skills/commands/agents: use **`/audit-skill`**, **`/audit-slash-command`**, or **`/audit-subagent`** before committing
-5. Use **`/add-to-todos`** to capture any follow-up items discovered during work
-Dev server rule: start `npm run dev` **only** to test UI changes; stop immediately after.
-Background process rule: kill all background shells (Playwright tests, long-running scripts, etc.) when done.
-- Check running shells: Use `/tasks` command or monitor system reminders
-- Kill shells: Use `KillShell` tool with shell_id
-**ALL prompts are automatically analyzed and routed to optimal tache features based on:**
-- **Complexity**: Simple (1-2 files) → direct; Moderate (3-10 files) → delegate; Complex (multi-stage) → pipeline
-**Routing is transparent—just describe what you need naturally.**
-| "Plan the X feature" | `/create-plan` | Creates hierarchical plan with phases |
-| "Debug intermittent Y" | `debug-like-expert` skill | Systematic root cause analysis |
-| "Should we use A or B?" | Appropriate `/consider:*` | Structured decision framework |
-| "Refactor across N files" | `/create-prompt` + `/run-prompt` | Delegates to fresh context |
-### Standard Workflow (for direct execution or after routing)
-1. **Plan:** For complex work, routing auto-suggests `/create-plan`. Otherwise create `tasks/<descriptive-name>.md` (stage early). Include:
-2. **Approval gate:** **must use `ExitPlanMode` before any code edits/writes**.
-3. **Execute:** use `TodoWrite`; mark items as you finish them; keep commits atomic.
-4. **Verify:** automated + manual checks for impacted UX flows.
-5. **Close out:** add task "Review" section (what changed, verification, issues).
-Pre‑commit: commits are blocked when code changes without a staged task file (see `.husky/pre-commit`).
-**Core Principle:** We build features, we use utilities. Never copy-paste complete feature implementations.
-- All feature logic, business rules, UI/UX
-  - Examples: D3 (graph layout), Recharts (chart primitives), Lexical (rich text editing), Konva (canvas rendering), XTerm (terminal emulation)
-  - Criterion: We own the feature, library handles complexity we shouldn't reimplement
-  - Examples: Full Excalidraw (diagram editor), Trello clone (task board), complete calendar apps
-  - Criterion: If we're wrapping/extending their feature instead of building our own, it's wrong
-- **Must use:** MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD licenses (commercial use allowed)
-- **Never use:** GPL, AGPL (copyleft - blocks future commercialization)
-- **Verify:** All dependencies must allow selling the platform for profit
-1. **Am I building or wrapping?** If wrapping → reject, build custom
-2. **What % is my code?** If <80% → reject, build custom or use smaller utility
-3. **Can I sell this?** If license blocks commercial use → reject
-4. **Can I fork if needed?** If closed-source or unmaintained → reject
-5. **Bundle size?** If >100KB for single feature → reject or find smaller alternative
-- ✅ **D3 for Graph View:** We use D3's force simulation (utility), we built the entire Graph feature
-- ✅ **Konva for Diagrams:** We use Konva for canvas (utility), we built the entire Diagram editor
-- ✅ **Recharts for Analytics:** We use Recharts primitives (utility), we built the Analytics feature
-- ❌ **Full Excalidraw:** Would be 90% their code, 10% ours - this is wrong
-- ❌ **Trello library:** Would be wrapping their task board, not building ours
-Can we truthfully say "We built this feature" and have full control to add any capability we want? If no, wrong approach.
-- Build: `npm run build`, `npm run build:production` (if present)
-- Test: `npm run test`, `npm run test:ui` (if present), `npm run test:coverage` (if present)
-- Quality: `npm run lint`, `npm run format`, `npm run preview` (if present)
-**Unit Tests (Vitest):** Run automatically in CI on every push. Fast, reliable, comprehensive coverage.
-**E2E Tests (Playwright):** Run **locally only**, not in CI. Rationale:
-- Local-first app architecture = testing where app runs (locally) makes most sense
-- No server/deployment to verify in CI
-- E2E tests more valuable during local development
-**Before committing:** Run `npm run test && npm run type-check` locally. Optionally run `npx playwright test` for E2E verification of critical flows.
-- **Total initial load:** keep as low as possible via lazy routes/widgets; explain tradeoffs when exceeded.
-- Must use lazy-loading for heavy pages/widgets and prefer named imports (tree‑shaking).
-UX targets: <1s load where feasible; <100ms interaction latency where feasible; WCAG AA minimum.

-- **No DOM manipulation:** Use refs and effect callbacks, not `document.querySelector`.

-Service modules under `src/services/`. Services encapsulate business logic, I/O, validation, and external integrations. They are framework-agnostic (no React, no DOM).
-- **No UI dependencies:** Services must not import React, DOM APIs, or UI components.
-- **Strong typing:** Use explicit TypeScript types + Zod schemas. No `any`.
-- **Validate I/O:** Parse all inputs/outputs with Zod. Never silently coerce.
-- **No silent failures:** Always propagate errors. Never `catch` and ignore.
-- **Secrets via env:** Load from `import.meta.env`, never hardcode.
-- **Stateless default:** If stateful (e.g., WebSocket), provide explicit cleanup API.
-**For service failures** (network errors, validation errors, unexpected behavior):

-- Example: "IndexedDB transaction fails intermittently when saving to TimeTrackingStore"

-- Use **`/consider:second-order`** to analyze ripple effects and downstream impacts
-- Use **`/consider:10-10-10`** for long-term implications of service changes
-- Use **`/consider:5-whys`** to drill to root cause and prevent recurrence

-This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
-1) First think through the problem, read the codebase for relevant files, and write a plan to a tasks/todo.md file.
-2) The plan should have a list of todo items that you can check off as you complete them.
-3) Before you begin working, check in with me and I will verify the plan.
-4) Then, begin working on the todo items, marking them as complete as you go.
-5) Please - every step of the way - just give me a high level explanation of what changes you made.
-6) Make every task and code change you do as simple as possible. We want to avoid making any massive or complex changes.
-7) Finally, add a review section to the tasks/todo.md file you made at the beginning of this plan (Step 1) with a summary of the changes you made and any other relevant information.
-8) DO NOT BE LAZY. NEVER BE LAZY. IF THERE IS A BUG FIND THE ROOT CAUSE AND FIX IT. NO TEMPORARY FIXES. YOU ARE A SENIOR DEVELOPER. NEVER BE LAZY.

-9) MAKE ALL FIXES AND CODE CHANGES AS SIMPLE AS HUMANLY POSSIBLE. THEY SHOULD ONLY IMPACT NECESSARY CODE RELEVANT TO THE TASK AND NOTHING ELSE. IT SHOULD IMPACT AS LITTLE CODE AS POSSIBLE. YOUR GOAL IS TO NOT INTRODUCE ANY BUGS. IT'S ALL ABOUT SIMPLICITY.

-10) IF SOMETHING HAPPENS THAT REQUIRES A CHANGE, UPDATE, ADDITION OR A COMPLETELY NEW MD FILE TO THE DOCUMENTATION YOU HAVE CREATED THEN BE SURE TO DO THAT. THESE DOCUMENTS SHOULD REMAIN INCREDIBLY DETAILED, CLEAR AND CLEAN.

-11) Make sure you are working off the most up to date version of the main branch of the codebase at the start of EVERY conversation/session. Run: `git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit`

-12) **Perfect Foundation Mindset** - This project is ~1% complete. EVERY decision must build the perfect foundation for the other 99%. This applies to EVERYTHING: documentation organization (files in optimal locations), code architecture (built for infinite scalability), feature design (seamless integration, right primitives), and overall execution (flawless, not just "it works"). See "Perfect Foundation Principle" section below for details.

-13) **Complete Logical Units of Work** - Once you start a task, continue until it reaches a natural stopping point. Do NOT stop mid-implementation or leave broken code. Complete the current logical unit (phase, feature, or clean commit) before pausing. If approaching token limits, commit what's complete and create clear handoff notes. Natural stopping points: all phases complete, all tests passing, documentation updated, clean git state. This ensures code is always in a working state and prevents context loss between sessions.

-14) **Validate Current Date** - Always check `<env>` section for "Today's date" before adding timestamps to any files (Privacy policies, task files, documentation headers, git commit messages). The `<env>` section contains the accurate current date - NEVER guess or assume. When modifying files with "Last updated" fields, verify the date is accurate.

-15) **Commit and Push Completed Work** - At the end of EVERY completed task/session, commit all changes and push to main branch. The commit message must have a descriptive title and a very detailed body explaining everything that was done. Run: `git add .`, then `git commit` with comprehensive message (see Git Workflow section), then `git push origin main`. This ensures all work is saved, tracked, and available for future sessions. DO NOT skip this step - it's as critical as Rule #11 (syncing at start).

-The system uses a **manifest architecture** centered on `assets/statistics/manifest.json`:
-- **Registry Pattern**: Manifest tracks all season files, metadata, and file hashes
-- **Lazy Loading**: Season data loaded on-demand via `window.codexData.getSeasonData(year)`
-- **Caching**: In-memory Map caches prevent redundant fetches
-- **IIFE Module**: `data.js` wraps utilities in immediately-invoked function, exposing global `window.codexData`
-**Critical Pattern**: Yahoo team IDs change between seasons. The franchise identity system maintains stable identities.
-- **Canonical Slugs**: Each franchise has a stable slug (e.g., "mega-powers", "dream-team")
-- **Alias Resolution**: Maps Yahoo team names and manager names to franchise slugs
-- **Theming**: Each franchise has `primaryColor` and `secondaryColor` for CSS variables
-- **Identity Matching**: Algorithms match Yahoo data to franchises via team names, manager names, or patterns
-When processing Yahoo data, always resolve to franchise slugs rather than using raw Yahoo team IDs.
-- `teams.raw.json` - Raw Yahoo API response for teams
-CSV files serve as fallback format when JSON is unavailable.

> AI assistant guidelines for the LazyGolfing monorepo

**Last Updated:** 2025-12-06

---

1. [Quick Reference](#quick-reference) - Commands, endpoints, test snippets

## ⚠️ Critical Development Priorities

> **These principles are NON-NEGOTIABLE.** Every decision, feature, and line of code must align with these priorities. When in conflict, priorities higher in the list take precedence.

### Priority Hierarchy (In Order)

#### 1. 🔒 SECURITY (Non-Negotiable)
- **Defense in depth** - Multiple layers of protection, never rely on single control
- **Never trust user input** - Validate, sanitize, escape at every boundary
- **Secure by default** - New features must be secure out of the box
- **Zero tolerance for secrets in code** - No API keys, tokens, passwords in source
- **OWASP Top 10 awareness** - Actively prevent XSS, injection, CSRF, auth issues
- **Data minimization** - Only collect/store what's absolutely necessary
- **Audit logging** - Track security-relevant actions for forensics

#### 2. 🏗️ FOUNDATIONAL (Build Right)
- **Solid architecture first** - No shortcuts that create tech debt
- **Single source of truth** - One authoritative location for every piece of data
- **Clean integration** - New features must integrate with existing systems cleanly
- **Backward compatibility** - Don't break existing functionality without migration path
- **Database integrity** - Proper constraints, indexes, foreign keys, cascades

#### 3. 🛡️ STABILITY (Never Crash)
- **Zero tolerance for crashes** - Comprehensive error handling everywhere
- **No data loss** - Every user action either succeeds completely or fails safely
- **Graceful degradation** - Features fail gracefully when dependencies unavailable
- **Test critical paths** - Auth, payments, data mutations must have test coverage
- **Defensive programming** - Assume inputs are malformed, APIs are down, state is corrupted

#### 4. ✨ UX/UI (User Delight)
- **Speed is a feature** - Target <100ms perceived interaction latency
- **Mobile-first responsive** - Design for mobile, enhance for desktop
- **Intuitive flows** - Users should never wonder "what do I do next?"
- **Accessibility required** - WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, keyboard nav, screen readers
- **Consistent design language** - Miami Vice theme applied uniformly
- **Feedback on every action** - Loading states, success messages, error explanations
- **Forgiveness** - Easy undo, confirmation for destructive actions

#### 5. 🎯 LEAN (Ship Minimal)
- **MVP first** - Ship the minimum viable solution, iterate based on feedback
- **No feature creep** - If it's not solving a real user problem, don't build it
- **Question every dependency** - Each package adds risk, size, and maintenance burden
- **Simpler is better** - Fewer abstractions, less code, easier maintenance
- **Delete before adding** - Remove unused code before adding new features

#### 6. 🧹 CLEAN (Readable Code)
- **Readable > clever** - Anyone should understand code without deep context
- **Consistent naming** - Follow established conventions throughout
- **Single responsibility** - Each function/component does one thing well
- **No dead code** - Remove unused imports, functions, files immediately
- **Self-documenting** - Code explains "what", comments explain "why"

#### 7. 📈 RETENTION (Bring Users Back)
- **Valuable data** - Help users track stats they can't get elsewhere
- **Progress visualization** - Show improvement over time
- **Social features** - Playing partners, comparisons, friendly competition
- **Low friction** - One-tap round logging, smart defaults, remember preferences
- **Notifications done right** - Valuable reminders, never spam

#### 8. ⚡ EFFICIENT + OPTIMIZED (Performance)
- **Measure first** - Profile before optimizing, no premature optimization
- **Performance budgets** - Set and enforce bundle size limits
- **Core Web Vitals** - LCP < 2.5s, FID < 100ms, CLS < 0.1
- **Database optimization** - Proper indexes, query analysis, N+1 prevention
- **Caching strategy** - Cache at every layer (CDN, API, database, client)
- **Lazy loading** - Load code and data only when needed

#### 9. 🔄 UPDATED + KNOWLEDGEABLE (Stay Current)
- **Current dependencies** - Regular updates, security patches applied promptly
- **Modern patterns** - Use current best practices, avoid deprecated approaches
- **Understand before changing** - Read existing code thoroughly before modifying
- **Know the "why"** - Understand reasoning behind architectural decisions
- **Documentation current** - CLAUDE.md and docs updated with every change

#### 10. 🏌️ INDUSTRY-FAMILIAR (Golf Domain Expertise)
- **Handicap systems** - GHIN, WHS, course/slope ratings, handicap index calculation
- **Golf terminology** - Correct use of GIR, FIR, putts per round, scoring terms
- **Course data** - Par, rating, slope, tee boxes, layouts
- **Tournament formats** - Stroke play, match play, Stableford, scramble
- **Competitor awareness** - Know what GHIN, Golf Pad, 18Birdies offer

---

### Golden Rules (Never Break)

1. **CSV is source of truth** - Never edit JSON in `/data/` directly
2. **Always run `npx prisma generate`** after schema.prisma changes
3. **Protected routes need `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard)`**
4. **Admin routes need `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard, AdminGuard)`**
5. **All inputs validated** via class-validator DTOs
6. **Never commit secrets** - Check `.env`, API keys, tokens
7. **Test before deploy** - No untested code reaches production
8. **Mobile-first** - Every feature must work on mobile devices

### Before Completing ANY Task

**Quick checklist** (see `@docs/guidelines/COMPLETION_CHECKLISTS.md` for detailed checklists):

- [ ] **Security:** No vulnerabilities (injection, XSS, auth bypass, secrets)
- [ ] **Stability:** Error handling for all failure modes
- [ ] **UX:** Works on mobile, accessible, has loading/error states
- [ ] **Lean:** No unnecessary code, dependencies, or features
- [ ] **Clean:** Follows existing patterns, readable, no dead code
- [ ] **Tests:** `npm run test` (backend) / `npm run build` (frontend) pass
- [ ] **Docs:** CLAUDE.md updated if patterns changed

### CSV Data Workflow

```bash
# 1. Edit CSV file
vim golfers/golfers.csv

# 2. Regenerate JSON
npm run build:data

# 3. Verify
npm run check:data

# 4. Commit BOTH files
git add golfers/golfers.csv data/golfers.json
git commit -m "data(golfers): update..."
```

## Architecture Overview

### Frontend Data Flow

```
CSV files (source of truth)     JSON (build artifact)       Browser
golfers/golfers.csv      -->    data/golfers.json     -->   fetch + render
leagues/leagues.csv      -->    data/leagues.json
tours/tours.csv          -->    data/tours.json
```

### Backend Module Structure

```
src/
├── auth/           # Google OAuth + JWT (cookie: lg_jwt)
├── profile/        # GET/PUT /v1/me
├── rounds/         # CRUD /v1/rounds
├── courses/        # Course management
├── prisma/         # Database service (global module)
└── audit-log/      # Action logging
```

### Database Models

| Model | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `User` | OAuth identity (Google), 1:1 with Player |
| `Player` | Golf profile (handicap, GHIN), has many Rounds |
| `Round` | Golf rounds with stats |
| `Course` | Course data with API integration |
| `RoundLink` | Playing partner linking |
| `Role/UserRole` | RBAC |
| `AuditLog` | Action tracking |

### Authentication Flow

1. Frontend → `GET /auth/google` → Google consent
2. Google → Backend callback with code
3. Backend creates/updates User + Player
4. Backend signs JWT, sets `lg_jwt` HttpOnly cookie
5. Backend redirects to frontend success page
6. Frontend calls `/auth/session` to confirm

## AI Working Principles

### Approach

1. **Read first, act later** - Understand existing code before modifying
2. **Follow patterns** - Match the style of surrounding code
3. **Test before completing** - Run tests, verify manually
4. **Security first** - Never expose secrets, validate inputs

### When to Ask

- Requirements are ambiguous
- Security implications unclear
- Breaking changes needed
- Performance trade-offs exist

### When to Proceed

- Implementation matches existing patterns
- Non-breaking additions
- Bug fixes with clear root cause
- Refactoring that preserves behavior

# CLAUDE.md - Backend (lzg.backend)

> AI assistant guidelines for the NestJS API backend

**Last Updated:** 2025-12-11

## ⚠️ Critical Priorities (Backend API)

> **See [/CLAUDE.md](../CLAUDE.md) for the complete priority hierarchy.** This section covers backend-specific applications of those principles.

### Backend Priority Applications

| Priority | Backend Implementation |
|----------|----------------------|
| 🔒 **Security** | JWT + CSRF on all mutations, input validation via DTOs, parameterized queries (Prisma), rate limiting, never log secrets, RBAC with AdminGuard |
| 🏗️ **Foundational** | Prisma as single DB interface, NestJS module structure, consistent error responses, proper database constraints/indexes |
| 🛡️ **Stability** | Try-catch on all async, transaction rollbacks, connection pooling, graceful shutdown, health checks |
| ✨ **UX/UI** | Fast API responses (<200ms), meaningful error messages, proper HTTP status codes, pagination on lists |
| 🎯 **Lean** | No unused endpoints, minimal dependencies, efficient queries (avoid N+1), lazy load relations |
| 🧹 **Clean** | Service/Controller separation, DTOs for all inputs, consistent naming, typed Prisma queries |
| ⚡ **Performance** | Database indexes on query fields, query optimization, connection pooling, response caching where safe |
| 📊 **Observable** | Audit logging for mutations, structured error logging, PM2 monitoring |

### Backend Checklist (Every Change)

**Quick checklist** (see `@../docs/guidelines/COMPLETION_CHECKLISTS.md` for detailed checklists):

- [ ] Protected routes have `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard)`
- [ ] Admin routes have `@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard, AdminGuard)`
- [ ] All inputs validated via class-validator DTOs
- [ ] User can only access their own data (no IDOR)
- [ ] No secrets in code or logs
- [ ] `npm run test` passes
- [ ] `npx prisma generate` run after schema changes

### Security Non-Negotiables

```typescript
// ✅ ALWAYS: Protected mutation endpoint
@Post()
@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard)
async create(@Body() dto: CreateDto, @CurrentUser() user: User) {
  // Validate ownership in service layer
}

// ✅ ALWAYS: Admin-only endpoint
@Delete(':id')
@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard, AdminGuard)
async delete(@Param('id') id: string) { }

// ❌ NEVER: Unprotected mutation
@Post()
async create(@Body() dto: CreateDto) { }  // NO GUARDS = SECURITY HOLE
```

## Task Completion Workflow

**MANDATORY:** After completing any task or feature, follow this workflow.

### 1. Verify Build & Tests
```bash
npm run build
npm run test
```

### 2. Commit Changes
```bash
git status
git add -A
git commit -m "<type>(<scope>): <description>"
```

**Commit types:** `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `style`, `refactor`, `perf`, `test`, `chore`

### 3. Push to Main
```bash
git push origin main
```

### 4. Build AND Restart API
```bash
npm run build && pm2 restart lg-api
pm2 logs lg-api --lines 20
```

**⚠️ CRITICAL:** You MUST run `npm run build` before `pm2 restart`:
- PM2 runs compiled JavaScript from `dist/` folder
- Without building, PM2 restarts with OLD compiled code
- Your source changes will NOT take effect without building first

**Also check for changes in:**
- `/home/lzg/lazygolfing-frontend/lzg.home/` - Frontend repository
- `/home/lzg/` - Root docs and CLAUDE.md files

## Module Structure

```
src/
├── main.ts                     # Bootstrap (CORS, Helmet, validation, cookies)
├── app.module.ts               # Root module + ThrottlerModule
├── config/
│   └── env.validation.ts       # Environment variable validation
├── auth/                       # Google OAuth + JWT + CSRF
│   ├── auth.controller.ts      # /auth/google, /auth/session, /auth/logout
│   ├── auth.service.ts         # findOrCreateUser, validateUser, ensureRoles
│   ├── google.strategy.ts      # Passport Google OAuth
│   ├── jwt.strategy.ts         # Passport JWT validation
│   ├── jwt-auth.guard.ts       # Guard for protected routes
│   ├── admin.guard.ts          # Guard for admin-only routes
│   ├── csrf.guard.ts           # CSRF double-submit protection
│   ├── csrf.util.ts            # CSRF token generation
│   └── current-user.decorator.ts  # @CurrentUser() decorator
├── profile/                    # User profile + stats
│   ├── profile.controller.ts   # GET/PUT /v1/me, /v1/me/stats, /v1/me/export
│   ├── profile.service.ts      # getProfile, updateProfile, getStats, export
│   └── update-profile.dto.ts   # Validation DTO
├── rounds/                     # Golf rounds + linking
│   ├── rounds.controller.ts    # CRUD /v1/rounds + link endpoints
│   ├── rounds.service.ts       # Business logic + partner matching
│   └── dto/                    # create-round.dto.ts, update-round.dto.ts, import-csv.dto.ts
```

## Database Schema

### Model Relationships

```
User (OAuth identity)
  ├── 1:1 Player (golf profile)
  │        └── 1:N Round
  │                  └── N:1 Course (optional)
  ├── 1:N Notification (user notifications)
  ├── 1:N GroupInvite (sent/received)
  ├── 1:N SideGameInvite (sent/received)
  ├── 1:N Friendship (requester/addressee)
  └── 1:N Challenge (creator/receiver)

Role ←─ N:N ─→ User (via UserRole)
AuditLog (standalone)
```

### Key Fields

| Model | Key Fields |
|-------|------------|
| User | email, provider, providerId, avatarUrl, lastLoginAt |
| Player | firstName, lastName, ghinNumber, handicap |
| Round | strokes, front9, back9, girPct, firPct, totalPutts, birdies, pars, bogeys |
| Course | name, city, state, totalPar, courseRating, slopeRating, apiCourseId |
| Notification | type, title, userId, isRead, actionUrl, resourceId |
| GroupInvite | groupId, inviteeId, inviterId, status, message |
| SideGameInvite | gameId, inviteeId, inviterId, status, message |
| Friendship | requesterId, addresseeId, status |
| Challenge | challengerId, challengedId, status, startDate, endDate |

---

## Authentication Flow

```
1. Frontend → GET /auth/google
2. Redirect to Google consent
3. Google → /auth/google/redirect with code
4. GoogleStrategy exchanges code for user info
5. auth.service.findOrCreateUser() → User + Player
6. Sign JWT, set lg_jwt cookie (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=None, 7 days)
7. Redirect to frontend success page
```

### Cookie Config

```typescript
{
  httpOnly: true,        // XSS protection
  secure: true,          // HTTPS only (production)
  sameSite: 'none',      // Cross-origin allowed
  maxAge: 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000,  // 7 days
}
```

### Protecting Routes

```typescript
// Basic JWT protection
@Get('protected-route')
@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard)
async myRoute(@CurrentUser() user: any) {
  // user contains JWT payload: { sub: userId, email }
}

// Admin-only route
@Put('admin-route')
@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard, CsrfGuard, AdminGuard)
async adminOnly(@CurrentUser() user: any) {
  // Only users with 'admin' role can access
}
```

---

## CSRF Protection

### Double-Submit Cookie Pattern

Protected endpoints require the `X-CSRF-Token` header to match the `csrf_token` cookie.

**Cookie:** Set at login alongside `lg_jwt`
**Header:** `X-CSRF-Token: <token-value>`
**Validation:** Constant-time comparison (prevents timing attacks)

```typescript
// Frontend request example
fetch('/v1/me', {
  credentials: 'include',  // Include cookies
  headers: {
    'X-CSRF-Token': getCookie('csrf_token')
  }
});
```

**Skip for:** GET, HEAD, OPTIONS requests

---

## Rate Limiting

Global rate limiting via `@nestjs/throttler`:

| Category | Limit | Window |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Default | 120 requests | 1 minute |
| Auth endpoints | 20 requests | 1 minute |
| Session check | 20 requests | 1 minute |

**Response when exceeded:** `429 Too Many Requests`

## Adding a New Endpoint

```typescript
// 1. Add DTO (src/[module]/dto/[action].dto.ts)
export class CreateThingDto {
  @IsString()
  @IsNotEmpty()
  name: string;
}

// 2. Add service method
async createThing(playerId: string, dto: CreateThingDto) {
  return this.prisma.thing.create({
    data: { ...dto, playerId },
  });
}

// 3. Add controller route
@Post()
@UseGuards(JwtAuthGuard)
async create(
  @Body() dto: CreateThingDto,
  @CurrentUser() user: any,
) {
  const profile = await this.profileService.getProfile(user.id);
  return this.thingService.createThing(profile.player.id, dto);
}

// 4. Write tests and run
npm run test
```

### Common Issues

| Symptom | Cause | Solution |
|---------|-------|----------|
| `Prisma Client not found` | Schema changed | `npx prisma generate` |
| Migration failed | DB not running | Check PostgreSQL status |
| 401 Unauthorized | Invalid JWT | Check cookie, re-login |

```bash
# 1. SSH to VM
# 2. Pull latest code
git pull origin main

npm ci
```

### Session Cleanup (MANDATORY)

**Before ending any session where services were modified:**

```bash
# Quick cleanup - ensures services auto-start on reboot
pm2 status && pm2 save && curl -s http://localhost:3000/ >/dev/null && echo "All services saved and running"
```

**Full procedure:**
```bash
# 1. Verify PM2 processes are running
pm2 status

# 2. Save process list (critical for auto-restore)
pm2 save

# 3. Verify services responding
curl -s http://localhost:3000/ && echo " - API OK"
pg_isready && echo " - PostgreSQL OK"
```
